Toxocara seropositivity is mentioned in the context of statistical analysis, but no prevalence data is provided.
70.Rationale: The passage does not provide seroprevalence data for Toxocara in children or establish a causal link between toxocariasis and epilepsy. It only states that toxocariasis may cause epilepsy, but no findings are presented to support it as the primary cause. The inference requires data not included in the Passage Body.

71.Option: Despite high geographical and therapeutic coverage of ivermectin mass drug administration, the continuous transmission of Onchocerca volvulus in the Mbam drainage basin is maintained by abundant blackflies with high vector capacity and efficiency, which can acquire microfilariae from infected individuals who do not comply with treatment or harbor parasite strains with sub-optimal response to ivermectin.
Evidence: Following the adoption of IVM-MDA as a control/elimination strategy in Cameroon, it has been demonstrated that the Community Directed Treatment with Ivermectin (CDTI) project of Central Region at the level of Bafia had limited impact on onchocerciasis in the human population despite more than 15 years of repeated annual treatment with IVM.
In some of the communities, about 60% of individuals screened for onchocerciasis were still harbouring Mf in their skin.
The presence of infection in humans even after numerous rounds of IVM treatment means that there is continuous transmission by the vectors.

Thus, this study was designed to investigate if there are factors that could have favoured the abundance, vector capacity and efficiency of Simulium damnusum for the transmission of onchocerciasis in this area and contribute to the persistence of disease despite a long-term effort for CDTI implementation.
Before IVM-MDA commenced (Day 0), 4,806 flies were collected in four days resulting in a daily biting rate of 1,201 flies/man/day.
Nine months after IVM-MDA (April 2017) 7,696 flies were collected with a daily biting rate of 1,539 flies/man/day.

The analysis of the Mbam river drainage revealed a very high rate of water discharge during both rainy and dry seasons in Bafia (study site) due to the presence of two dams on the Mbam tributaries (Noun and Mape) at Bamendjing and Mape, respectively. The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.
Kamga and colleagues observed a lower therapeutic coverage than that reported during the 2014 MDA campaign in the study site.
71.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that despite reported high therapeutic coverage of ivermectin MDA, onchocerciasis transmission persists in the Mbam basin. Human infection remains high (60% with microfilariae), indicating a reservoir for transmission. Entomological data show abundant blackflies with high biting rates and sustained infection/infective rates, supported by year-round breeding sites due to dams. The observation of lower actual therapeutic coverage implies non-compliance, which can sustain human infection and allow vectors to acquire microfilariae. While parasite strains with sub-optimal response are not mentioned, the 'or' in the option allows for non-compliance as a derivable factor from the coverage discrepancy. Thus, the inference about vector abundance and non-compliance as factors sustaining transmission is epidemiologically derivable from the evidence.

71.Option: The construction of the Mape and Bamendjing dams has led to a permanent increase in blackfly breeding sites, making onchocerciasis elimination impossible in the Mbam drainage basin without large-scale dam removal.
Evidence: The analysis of the Mbam river drainage revealed a very high rate of water discharge during both rainy and dry seasons in Bafia (study site) due to the presence of two dams on the Mbam tributaries (Noun and Mape) at Bamendjing and Mape, respectively. The high speed and volume of water plus the presence of rocks and vegetation in the river serves as excellent breeding site for blackflies in both the rainy and dry seasons of the year.
71.Rationale: While the Passage Body confirms that dams contribute to stable breeding sites for blackflies year-round, it does not state or imply that onchocerciasis elimination is impossible without dam removal. This is an overstatement not supported by the text; the study focuses on identifying factors but does not conclude on the necessity of dam removal for elimination. The inference exceeds what can be derived from the evidence provided.

71.Option: The high monthly transmission potential (MTP) observed at 6 and 9 months post-MDA indicates that ivermectin treatment is ineffective in reducing microfilarial load in the human population, leading to sustained transmission by vectors.
Evidence: If IVM is administered in large scale, there will be a potential reduction of microfilaria (Mf) in the human population as it is known that a single standard dose (150 μg/kg body weight) of IVM can induce 98–99% reduction in skin microfilarial (Mf) load of O. volvulus within one month of treatment.
Within the first three months post MDA, MTP dropped from 340 to 95 (72% reduction).

Monthly Transmission Potential (MTP) of O. volvulus infective larvae from flies to humans dropped from 340 L3H/man/month before IVM-MDA to 229 L3H/man/month after 30 days of treatment. The MTP further decreased to 95 L3H/man/month at D90 but drastically increased to 205 and 471 L3H/man/month at six and nine months post treatment, respectively.
71.Rationale: The Passage Body demonstrates that ivermectin is effective in reducing microfilarial load and MTP initially, as shown by the 72% reduction within three months. The subsequent rise in MTP at later time points may be due to factors such as seasonal variations in vector abundance or continued transmission from untreated individuals, but it does not imply that ivermectin is ineffective. In fact, the evidence supports its temporary efficacy. Therefore, the inference that ivermectin is ineffective is not derivable and contradicts the presented data.

71.Option: The detection of O. ochengi in two body pools at 9 months post-MDA suggests that animal Onchocerca species are a significant source of transmission in the Mbam drainage basin, contributing to the persistence of onchocerciasis despite ivermectin treatment.
Evidence: Using the triplex real-time PCR for the detection of O. ochengi parasites (larvae) in the Simulium damnosum, none of the 129 head pools were positive (0%) while two of the 129 body pools (1.6%) had positive signals. This gave us a zero infective rate and an infection rate of 1.4 (n = 129 pools of 100 flies with 127 negative pools) per 1,000 parous flies. The two pools that were positive for O. ochengi were collected at 9 months post MDA in April 2017.
71.Rationale: The detection of O. ochengi is minimal (only two body pools, no infective pools) and the infection rate is very low (1.4 per 1,000 parous flies). There is no evidence that O. ochengi contributes significantly to onchocerciasis transmission, as it is not infective in this study and prevalence is negligible. The Passage Body mentions that animal species could be mistaken for O. volvulus, but the low detection rate does not support 'significant source of transmission.' Thus, this inference is not derivable from the evidence.

72.Option: Healthcare workers who perform skin biopsies on patients with cutaneous leishmaniasis are at risk of occupational infection through accidental needlestick injuries, and clinicians should consider this route of transmission when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in such individuals.
Evidence: We present a case of an accidental direct inoculation of Leishmania panamensis.

a 29-year-old otherwise healthy dermatology resident treated a Haitian immigrant patient with a presumptive diagnosis of an infectious dermatosis. She performed a skin biopsy on the patient and suffered an accidental needlestick injury on the left index finger.
In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation.
A skin biopsy was compatible with leishmaniasis.
72.Rationale: The passage directly describes a case where a healthcare worker performing a skin biopsy on a patient with leishmaniasis suffered a needlestick injury and subsequently developed cutaneous leishmaniasis at the inoculation site. This provides clear evidence of occupational risk through accidental inoculation. The inference that clinicians should consider this transmission route when evaluating unexplained skin lesions in healthcare workers with similar exposures is a logical epidemiological conclusion derived from the case details.

72.Option: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has guidelines for preventing the transmission of infectious diseases in the healthcare workplace, including needlestick and other sharps-related injury prevention programs, which should be strictly followed to prevent occupational leishmaniasis.
Evidence: The protocol for the management of needlestick and sharps-related injuries was implemented;
72.Rationale: While the passage mentions that a protocol for needlestick injury management was implemented, it does not mention the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) or any specific guidelines from that organization. The inference about CDC guidelines, while potentially true in a broader context, cannot be derived from the passage body as it relies on external knowledge not presented in the text.

72.Option: The successful treatment of the resident with meglumine antimoniate and intralesional injections indicates that cutaneous leishmaniasis following occupational exposure is inherently less severe than the mucocutaneous form seen in the patient.
Evidence: The Haitian patient was diagnosed with mucocutaneous leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania panamensis, and treatment was established with liposomal amphotericin B at 3 mg/kg per day intravenously (IV) for 10 days, followed by meglumine antimoniate at 20 mg/kg per day IV for 28 days, with complete remission.

A 20-day treatment with meglumine antimoniate, at 20 mg/kg per day IV, was established. After completing treatment, a small defect remained, so three additional doses of 1 ml intralesional meglumine antimoniate were administered. A week after the last injection, the ulcer had healed completely.
72.Rationale: The passage describes different treatment regimens for the patient (mucocutaneous form) and the resident (cutaneous form), both with successful outcomes. However, it does not provide any evidence comparing the inherent severity of the two forms or that occupational exposure leads to less severe disease. The difference in treatment could be due to various factors (e.g., disease presentation, clinical judgment) but does not support a conclusion about inherent severity based solely on this single case.

72.Option: The delayed onset of symptoms two months after the needlestick injury suggests that the resident’s immune system was unable to control the Leishmania infection initially, leading to a prolonged incubation period and eventual clinical manifestation.
Evidence: In January 2022, 2 months after the puncture, the resident developed an erythematous, painless papule at the point of inoculation.
72.Rationale: The passage reports the timeline of symptom onset (2 months post-injury) but provides no information about the resident's immune response or the reasons for the delay. While a prolonged incubation period is observed, the inference that the immune system was initially unable to control the infection is speculative and not directly supported by the passage. The delay could be due to various factors not discussed, such as parasite load or intrinsic incubation characteristics.

73.Option: The observed clinical signs and onset of disease are alike with what has been described for HPS in humans and the ANDV model in Syrian hamsters, suggesting that the immune system plays a central role in disease development through overactivation.
Evidence: The second phase starts rather abrupt and mortality is mostly seen within 24 hours after the onset of respiratory symptoms (pulmonary edema, respiratory distress, shock, cardiac failure).
The onset of disease was abrupt and mortality occurred within 24–36 hours after the first signs.
Starting 11 days post infection, hamsters died or showed signs of respiratory distress and a human-like disease pattern [13].
73.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the clinical course in immunosuppressed hamsters resembles human HPS and the ANDV hamster model in terms of abrupt onset and rapid mortality. However, the passage does not support the inference that the immune system's role is through 'overactivation.' Instead, the experimental results show that immunosuppression leads to lethal disease, suggesting that an intact immune system is protective against most hantaviruses. The concept of immune overactivation as a driver of pathology is not derived from the presented data.

73.Option: The passive immunization experiment confirms that the immune system is responsible for the observed disease pathology, as neutralizing antibodies prevented mortality in infected hamsters.
Evidence: Passive immunization was carried out to confirm that the virus was responsible for the observed disease and mortality.
In the test groups, receiving the BAYV and BCCV antibodies, all 8 hamsters in each group were still alive at the end of the experiment on day 35 post infection.
Both control groups showed a 100% mortality rate between 11 and 17 days post infection.
73.Rationale: The passive immunization experiment demonstrates that neutralizing antibodies against the virus prevent mortality, confirming that the virus itself is responsible for disease and death. This result indicates a protective role of the adaptive immune response (antibodies) rather than implicating the immune system as the cause of pathology. The option incorrectly interprets the findings as evidence that the immune system causes disease pathology.

73.Option: The fact that immunosuppressive agents are able to make Syrian hamsters vulnerable for hantaviruses other than Andes virus indicates that an interesting and probably complicated interplay between the virus and the immune system is responsible for the occurrence of hantavirus disease and its associated mortality.
Evidence: Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.
The scope of the current study is to further explore the use of immunosuppressive treatment to achieve an animal disease model for New World hantaviruses.
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality

For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed... for Bayou virus (BAYV)... for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV)... for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV)... and for SNV... For Choclo virus (CHOV), Laguna Negra virus (LNV), and Maporal virus (MAPV), respectively 75%, 50% and 50% mortality was observed.
73.Rationale: The passage shows that immunocompetent hamsters are resistant to most New World hantaviruses except ANDV, but when treated with immunosuppressive agents, they become susceptible to lethal infection by multiple viruses. This directly indicates that the host immune system normally controls infection and that its suppression alters disease outcome, implying a complex virus-immune interaction that determines disease occurrence and mortality.

73.Option: The lack of mortality in immunocompetent Syrian hamsters infected with New World hantaviruses other than Andes virus suggests that viral pathogenicity is primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors.
Evidence: Surprisingly, only ANDV was potentially lethal for hamsters whereas other New World hantaviruses showed no such outcomes.

For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed... for Bayou virus (BAYV)... for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV)... for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV)... and for SNV... For Choclo virus (CHOV), Laguna Negra virus (LNV), and Maporal virus (MAPV), respectively 75%, 50% and 50% mortality was observed.
73.Rationale: While the lack of disease in immunocompetent hamsters (except with ANDV) highlights the importance of host immunity, the variation in mortality rates among different hantaviruses under identical immunosuppressive conditions (e.g., 100% for ANDV/BAYV/BCCV vs. 50% for LNV/MAPV) demonstrates that viral genetic factors also significantly influence pathogenicity. The claim that pathogenicity is 'primarily determined by host-specific immune responses rather than viral genetic factors' is not supported, as both factors are evident from the results.

73.Option: The use of immunosuppressive agents enables the induction of lethal disease in Syrian hamsters infected with multiple New World hantaviruses, indicating that the immune system plays a critical role in modulating disease severity and that viral pathogenicity is not solely determined by the virus itself.
Evidence: SNV was previously tested in immunocompetent hamsters but did not cause any disease[13].
Infection of immunosuppressed hamsters with New World hantaviruses results in high mortality
For ANDV, 100% mortality was observed... for Bayou virus (BAYV)... for Black Creek Canal virus (BCCV)... for Caño Delgadito virus (CDV)... and for SNV...
73.Rationale: The experimental results directly show that immunosuppression converts non-lethal infections (e.g., SNV) into lethal ones, demonstrating that the host immune system is a critical modulator of disease severity. This implies that viral pathogenicity is not an intrinsic property of the virus alone but depends on host factors, specifically immune competence.

74.Option: The variation in infection prevalence between villages over time is best explained by differences in treatment coverage and local socioeconomic factors.
Evidence: The 16 villages varied widely in their pre-treatment and post-treatment prevalence, and in how rapidly infection returned after treatments had been discontinued.
The mean coverage of antibiotic at a village visit was 94.1% relative to the census, ranging from 73.9% to 100% of those eligible for treatment (Table 1).
74.Rationale: While the passage notes variation in prevalence and coverage, it does not provide evidence that these factors are the best explanation for the observed variation. The passage does not analyze or state that treatment coverage or local socioeconomic factors (beyond a general mention in the introduction that trachoma decreases in areas with socioeconomic improvements) explain the variation in this study. Correlation between baseline and 6-month prevalence is mentioned, but no direct link to coverage or socioeconomic factors is made for the post-discontinuation period.

74.Option: The return of infection after treatment discontinuation suggests that the antibiotic resistance is developing in the chlamydial population.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
74.Rationale: The passage documents the return of infection after treatment discontinuation, but it does not mention or suggest that antibiotic resistance is the cause. The observed return is attributed to natural recurrence or reinfection, not resistance. No data on antibiotic susceptibility or resistance mechanisms are presented or inferred.

74.Option: Infection may never return if it is brought to a low enough level, analogous to the Allee effect found in population biology.
Evidence: Even after four biannual distributions, infection returned after treatments were discontinued: 'From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2%'.
In the study villages, infection increased in 14 of 16 villages after the last treatment, indicating resurgence even when prevalence was reduced to 2.6%.
74.Rationale: The passage shows that infection returned after discontinuation even when prevalence was reduced to very low levels (2.6% on average). There is no evidence in the passage supporting the idea that bringing infection to a low enough level prevents return; instead, the data demonstrate recurrence. The Allee effect is not mentioned or implied.

74.Option: Even after four biannual distributions of azithromycin, infection predictably returns in trachoma-endemic communities, indicating that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without risk of resurgence.
Evidence: Infection increased in the 24 months after the last treatment in 14 of 16 villages, but remained lower than it had been before the first treatment in all 16 villages.
From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
At 24 months after the last treatment, the mean prevalence of infection was far higher than at 6 months after the last treatment (P=0.008), but was still only 40% of that at baseline (P<0.0001).
The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6%... but after discontinuation, infection returned.
74.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that after four biannual treatments, infection prevalence rebounded significantly within 24 months of discontinuation in most villages. This pattern indicates that discontinuing mass antibiotic distributions leads to resurgence of infection, supporting the inference that such distributions cannot be stopped without risk of recrudescence. The data show a predictable return of infection post-treatment, as seen in 14 of 16 villages.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated with P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4).

In contrast to other malaria species, most of the P. vivax costs were direct costs (63%) where hospital and clinic out-of-pocket costs were double the direct costs of malaria due to other Plasmodium species (Table E in S1 Text).
P. vivax (11.0 days) and P. falciparum (10.3 days) had a higher number of days affected by illness compared to P. knowlesi (8.7 days) and P. malariae (8.5 days).
76.Rationale: The passage provides data showing P. vivax has higher total costs and higher direct costs, but it does not provide information on treatment duration, frequency of clinic visits, or age profile specific to P. vivax patients. The number of days affected by illness is mentioned, but this is not linked to treatment duration or clinic visits. There is no evidence regarding a younger age profile for P. vivax patients; the mean age for all participants is 32 years, with no species-specific age breakdown. Therefore, the explanatory factors in the option are speculative and cannot be derived from the passage.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: Finally, when the household inequities survey for Sabah was utilized (US$389.21 per month) [31], the total cost increased by 78% (US$236.03) when applied to all adults in Scenario 4 and 104% (US$270.41) when applied to all patients in Scenario 5.
The wage used to estimate productivity losses greatly impacted the total cost per malaria episode.

Compared to the base case estimate of US$130.53 per person, the cost per episode decreased to US$114.30 when wage loss estimates were only applied to adults who reported their wages for Scenario 1 (Table 6). When using Sabah-specific estimates from the Malaysia Household Income survey (US$223.57 per month) applied to only adults in Scenario 2 [30], the total costs increased by 19% to US$158.85.
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that using standardized wage estimates from national surveys (specifically the household inequities survey) results in a 78% increase in total cost per episode compared to the base case. This sensitivity analysis shows that the base case estimate is sensitive to wage assumptions, and higher wage estimates lead to substantially higher costs, implying that the base case may underestimate the true economic burden. This inference is directly supported by the evidence from the scenario analysis section.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi is now the major cause of malaria in Malaysia, with 3000 to 4000 malaria case notifications per year since 2017 [12–14].
P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].
The total cost for working-age males was higher than the remainder of the participants, with a mean of US$146.34 (SD=106.90) versus US$112.01 (SD=93.88), respectively (p=0.006; Table D in S1 Text).
While direct costs were similar between working-age males and the rest of the population, working-age males reported higher indirect costs at US$95.66, compared to US$52.62 for others (p<0.001).
76.Rationale: The passage indicates that P. knowlesi is prevalent among working-age males and that working-age males have higher total costs, primarily due to higher indirect costs. However, direct costs are stated to be similar between working-age males and others, not increased. Therefore, the claim that both direct and indirect costs are increased is not supported. Additionally, while a financial burden is documented (e.g., catastrophic expenditure), the passage does not explicitly describe it as 'disproportionate' due to P. knowlesi transmission; the burden applies broadly to all malaria cases. Thus, the inference cannot be fully derived from the passage.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: Seven (5%) patients met the WHO research criteria for severe malaria; all were due to P. knowlesi infection.

The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.
The length of hospitalization for severe P. knowlesi cases (mean = 5.6 days, SD = 1.4) was higher than for non-severe malaria cases due to any species (mean = 3.9, SD = 1.4, p=0.004).
The average number of days lost to illness increased to 13.9 (SD = 15.6) for severe P. knowlesi cases, comparatively higher than non-severe P. knowlesi cases at 8.3 days (SD = 6.5, p = 0.141).
76.Rationale: The passage confirms that severe malaria cases are all P. knowlesi, have higher indirect costs, longer hospital stays, and greater productivity losses (days lost). However, it does not specify that these productivity losses are primarily among working-age males. While P. knowlesi is prevalent in working-age males, the data on severe cases do not break down by age or gender. Therefore, the addition of 'among working-age males' is not directly supported by the passage and requires an assumption beyond the provided evidence.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Malaysia has free universal access to malaria screening, testing, and treatment through public health facilities [6,7].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).
Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.

When using the mean monthly self-reported income value of US$148, direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income. Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.

When using a higher defined threshold for catastrophic health expenditure of 25% of monthly income, the direct costs of a malaria episode reported by 38% (57/152) of patients in this survey remained at levels consistent with catastrophic health expenditure.

When using the Malaysia household income survey data as a higher baseline mean monthly income value of US$223 [30], 62% (94/152) of patients reported health expenditure above 10% of monthly income. This percentage dropped to 24% (36/152) when applying the higher 25% monthly income defined threshold.
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that despite free clinical care, households incur out-of-pocket costs, with a mean total cost of approximately $131. It also demonstrates that a large proportion of households experience catastrophic health expenditure, especially when using lower income thresholds (e.g., self-reported income), where 80% exceed 10% of monthly income. The evidence directly supports all parts of this inference without requiring additional assumptions.

77.Option: Negative perceptions of tsetse traps are prone to change over time, particularly after exposure to the technology and through community education, leading to sustained acceptance and active participation in control efforts.
Evidence: However, despite describing these initial feelings of fear, surprise and curiosity, none of the participants of HAT+ group expressed having negative feelings about traps any more. On the contrary, all participants expressed positive attitudes towards traps, which indicates that attitudes are prone to change over time.
Similarly participants from HAT+ villages, communicated changed negative- initial attitudes...

Figure 3 illustrates acceptance of traps by HAT+ and HAT− group. The time dimension is plotted with past, current and potential future level of acceptance. Potential future has been evaluated through perceptions of the HAT+ group, in which once high acceptance of traps has been achieved in the past it remained stable over time.
Positive association of deployment-personnel with health workers prompted to similar reactions...
Being able to observe insects being trapped, which was mostly reported by female participants, also seemed to influence positive attitudes...
77.Rationale: The Passage directly states that attitudes toward traps change over time with exposure and information. HAT+ villages, which had prior exposure to traps, shifted from initial fear to sustained positive acceptance. The study describes how education and observing benefits lead to increased acceptance and willingness to participate, supporting the inference about long-term trajectory.

77.Option: The geographical proximity of HAT+ and HAT− villages suggests that information about tsetse control programs spreads easily between communities, leading to similar levels of acceptance over time.
Evidence: Villages from HAT+ and HAT− areas lie within 10 kilometres of each other. Villagers from different villages often mix in the local markets and during social events such as celebrations of country independence, women's day, Christmas; hence the differences in views described below were surprising.
77.Rationale: Although villages are close and villagers mix, the Passage highlights significant differences in knowledge and perceptions between HAT+ and HAT- villages, such as HAT+ villages having positive attitudes while HAT- villages had initial fear and suspicion. This indicates that proximity does not automatically lead to similar acceptance, and the differences were surprising, contradicting the option.

77.Option: The effectiveness of tsetse traps in reducing disease incidence is the main reason for community acceptance, regardless of prior exposure or cultural beliefs.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action...
Associations of traps with witchcraft and ghosts...
In the HAT− group this conclusion was arrived at mostly from observation of trapped insects... however, initial reactions were dominated by cultural beliefs.
77.Rationale: While effectiveness is mentioned as a factor in HAT+ villages, the Passage shows that initial acceptance in HAT- villages is heavily influenced by cultural and supernatural associations, not effectiveness. Acceptance develops over time with education and exposure, indicating that effectiveness alone is not the main reason regardless of other factors.

77.Option: Community willingness to participate in tsetse control is primarily driven by financial incentives, as evidenced by men's expressed interest in government involvement and financial motivation.
Evidence: In both groups women expressed more need for empowerment (see a quote below) and requested access to traps more often, while men expressed more need for village decision making, financial motivation and government involvement in tsetse control programs.
Regardless of the group, participants expressed willingness and appeared motivated to be involved in tsetse control programs...
77.Rationale: Financial motivation is mentioned for men, but the Passage does not state it as the primary driver. Willingness is expressed broadly and linked to factors like knowledge, experience with HAT, and empowerment, not primarily financial incentives. Thus, this option overstates the evidence.

77.Option: The initial fear and suspicion toward tsetse traps in HAT− villages are primarily due to the traps' appearance and color, rather than cultural or supernatural associations.
Evidence: The suspicions and fear were not reported to be in any way related to the appearance of traps, but rather its position next to the river, uncertainty about the person who carried out the deployment and the unknown purpose of this action...
Associations of traps with witchcraft and ghosts...

According to participants of both groups, these objects, activities and people could be associated with two forms of supernatural powers: i) witchcraft (Lugbara: “ojo azi ngazu”) and ii) ghosts from the river (Lugbara: “orindi onzi”; orindi-ghost/spirit, onzi-evil/bad).
77.Rationale: The Passage explicitly states that fear and suspicion are not due to appearance or color but are linked to cultural and supernatural associations, such as witchcraft and ghosts. Therefore, this option contradicts the evidence provided.

78.Option: The persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection indicates that the current WHO guidelines for trachoma elimination are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage.
Evidence: Unfortunately, there is often a mismatch between disease and infection: infection may detected without clinical disease and disease may be found without infection.
Overall, TF%1–9 declined during the study: reaching a minimum of 2.6% at 30 months and rising to 4.8% at five years.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years.
78.Rationale: The passage demonstrates a discrepancy between clinical signs (TF) and infection status, but it does not evaluate the effectiveness of WHO guidelines in preventing long-term ocular damage. The guidelines aim to reduce TF%1–9 below 5%, which was nearly achieved in the study. No evidence is provided on long-term ocular damage outcomes; the focus is on prevalence indicators, not clinical outcomes like blindness or corneal damage.

78.Option: The lack of a control group in the study design means that the observed decline in C. trachomatis infection cannot be attributed to mass drug administration, and therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
Evidence: We investigated the long-term effect of a single MDA on both the signs of trachoma and ocular C. trachomatis infection in a low prevalence setting in The Gambia.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years.

The risk of infection at any follow-up time point did not differ between those who had received treatment at baseline and those who had not or who were new residents during the follow-up (data not shown).
78.Rationale: While the study lacks a control group, the passage does not conclude that the decline cannot be attributed to MDA. In fact, it presents the decline following MDA and discusses its effect. The passage raises questions about efficiency in low prevalence settings but does not recommend against antibiotic use. The inference that treatment should not be used is an extreme conclusion not supported by the evidence provided.

78.Option: The observed re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal, suggesting that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.
Evidence: In contrast, in villages 1 and 3 the prevalence of infection increased at two months (Table 5). This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1) [9].
78.Rationale: This evidence directly links re-infection with travel to a large religious festival, implying that such gatherings can facilitate the reintroduction of C. trachomatis. The association is statistically strong, allowing the epidemiological inference that large-scale community gatherings may serve as hubs for transmission in low prevalence settings.

78.Option: The marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces observed during the study suggests that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
Evidence: There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years. Similarly there was a large reduction in the proportion of 1 to 9 year olds with unclean faces (nasal or ocular discharge present): 38.4% (95% CI 34.1 to 42.7) at baseline and 5.0% (95% CI 3.1 to 6.8) at 5 years.
The project team provided health education about trachoma to the communities involved throughout the course of the study.
78.Rationale: While reductions in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces were observed, and health education was provided, the passage does not isolate the effect of health education from other interventions (e.g., MDA, latrine installation, surgery). Multiple components of the SAFE strategy were implemented simultaneously, so it cannot be inferred that health education was the primary driver. No evidence is provided to rank the contributions of each intervention.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: Unfortunately, there is often a mismatch between disease and infection: infection may detected without clinical disease and disease may be found without infection.
in low prevalence settings where infection and disease are poorly correlated it may not represent the most efficient way to eliminate trachoma.

After the two-month follow-up TF%1–9 was found to exceed the 10% treatment threshold in several villages on one or more occasions (Table 4). However, in only 4/12 (33%) of these occasions were cases of chlamydial infection detected in the same village (Table 5).
Where TF%1–9 exceeds 10%, three annual MDAs followed by re-assessment, to determine whether to continue treating, is recommended [8].
78.Rationale: The passage clearly documents the discrepancy between clinical signs (TF) and infection status, especially in low prevalence settings. The WHO algorithm uses TF%1–9 >10% to trigger MDA, but the evidence shows that TF can exceed this threshold without active infection. Therefore, following this algorithm could result in MDA being administered to communities where infection is absent, making it unnecessary from an infection-control perspective.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized care stems from the belief that outpatient treatment is more effective in healing BU lesions than hospital-based care.
79.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that households believe outpatient treatment is more effective. In fact, the passage indicates that for advanced cases, hospital treatment is necessary, and some households choose outpatient care despite recognizing it may be suboptimal (e.g., Prisca's case where she accepts potential infirmity). The reasons for preferring decentralized care do not include perceived superior clinical efficacy.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment is primarily due to the greater availability of trained health staff at local health stations compared to centralized hospitals.
79.Rationale: The passage does not compare the availability of trained health staff between local health stations and hospitals. It describes health stations as staffed by nurses and hospitals as handling severe cases, but there is no statement suggesting that households prefer decentralized care because of greater staff availability at health stations.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because hospital-based treatment for BU is often delayed due to long waiting lists and limited bed availability.
79.Rationale: The passage does not mention long waiting lists or limited bed availability as factors influencing the preference for decentralized care. Delays in seeking treatment are discussed, but these are attributed to factors like perceptions of causality, fear of surgery, and costs, not hospital capacity constraints.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment over hospital care is driven not only by geographical accessibility but also by the need to avoid indirect and opportunity costs, preserve social capital, and mitigate social risks such as rumors about moral impropriety.
Evidence: First and foremost, decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household. Decentralized treatment, for all but very severe cases of BU, still enables a woman to do chores and watch her children as well as engage in entrepreneurial activities essential for household survival.
Second, decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
Third, it allows children to stay in school while being treated, and it reduces the need to remove children from school and apprenticeships to serve as patient caretakers.
Fourth, a mother does not have to worry about the quality of sibling care and foster care in her absence.
Fifth, she also does not have to worry about pernicious rumors undermining her own or a daughter’s reputation.
Sixth, remaining at home during treatment is less stressful. A woman worries less about abandonment.

The enabling factors most commonly referenced in interviews about hospital-based BU care were the indirect costs of “free treatment” such as transportation costs, food and incidental costs (soap, mobile phone credit, etc.), and the opportunity cost of lost labor.

A second notable concern that we identified as having a big effect on health care seeking and patient caretaker decision making was social risk (risk to reputation and to present and future social relationships) ... When a woman leaves the confines of her village either to visit a health post some distance away or to reside in a hospital, she risks becoming the subject of rumors about sexual indiscretion.

Volunteering to take children afflicted with BU to health stations for outpatient care, watching children when a mother is away from home, and lending money or supplying food were all found to be means of solidifying social bonds between women. This form of “bonding social capital” ... provides women with a safety net ... On the other hand, we found that when requests for time or resources exceeded the capacity of kin to provide, social bonds were weakened.
79.Rationale: The passage explicitly lists six reasons for preferring decentralized care, all of which align with avoiding indirect/opportunity costs, preserving social capital, and mitigating social risks. These factors are extensively discussed throughout the results sections, providing strong evidence that they are key underlying factors driving the preference for outpatient treatment.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because it allows for greater involvement of traditional healers in the treatment process, which strengthens cultural continuity and trust.
79.Rationale: The passage does not state that decentralized care facilitates greater involvement of traditional healers. Traditional healers are mentioned as potential members of therapy management groups, but the preference for decentralized care is attributed to practical household and social concerns, not to integration of traditional healing. The reasons given for preferring decentralized care do not include cultural continuity with traditional practices.

80.Option: The model's conclusion that full access to antivenom is cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims in ASEAN countries switching from traditional to conventional treatment, which may not be feasible in remote rural areas.
Evidence: In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.
80.Rationale: The passage states the assumption that victims switch from traditional to conventional treatment in the full access scenario, but it does not discuss the feasibility of this switch in remote rural areas, nor does it explicitly state that the model's conclusion is contingent on this assumption. The feasibility comment is an external inference not supported by the passage.

80.Option: Improving access to antivenom in ASEAN countries would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR.
Evidence: The lowest level of antivenom effectiveness, presented as a risk ratio of death in indicated victims who were not treated with antivenom compared to those treated with antivenom, was ranging from 0.35 in Lao PDR to 1.19 in Myanmar.
80.Rationale: While threshold values are provided, the passage does not state that cost-effectiveness requires exceeding these values. Moreover, the interpretation of these thresholds is complex; for Lao PDR, a value below 1 contradicts the base-case assumption of antivenom effectiveness. The option's claim is not directly derivable from the passage.

80.Option: The high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, including 243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths annually, underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production to meet demand.
Evidence: Our previous study estimated that there were approximately 243,000 snakebite victims with 16,000 deaths and 950 amputations from snakebite envenoming in seven ASEAN countries
improving access to antivenoms could not be solely done by increasing the production of antivenoms.
80.Rationale: Although the high burden is stated, the passage explicitly notes that increasing antivenom production alone is insufficient and that a multifaceted approach is needed. Therefore, the inference that there is an urgent need for increased production is not directly supported by the passage.

80.Option: The inclusion of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model significantly increased the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per DALY averted, making the intervention less cost-effective.
Evidence: Scenario analyses incorporating PTSD in the model... resulted in similar ICERs per death averted and ICERs per DALY averted in all countries indicating that improving access will result in cost-saving.
80.Rationale: The passage indicates that including PTSD did not significantly increase ICERs; instead, it resulted in similar ICERs, meaning the intervention remained cost-saving. Therefore, the option's claim that PTSD inclusion significantly increased ICERs is contrary to the passage.

80.Option: Improving access to snake antivenom from the current level to full access in ASEAN countries would result in cost-saving despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses.
Evidence: Full access to antivenom had higher direct costs (2 million USD in Philippines to 50 million USD in Indonesia) but less indirect costs (-1,091 million USD in Indonesia to -28 million USD in Philippines) when compared to current access which resulted in total cost savings of 27 million USD in Philippines to 1,040 million USD in Indonesia.
improving access to snake antivenom will result in cost-saving.
80.Rationale: The passage directly states that full access leads to cost savings due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses, despite higher direct costs. This is a clear inference from the projected outcomes.

81.Option: The FP assay's ability to detect compound binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence from nematode extracts, which necessitates the use of complex normalization procedures in drug screening.
Evidence: Rewardingly, cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species (Figure 1A) with a high affinity, similar to the tumor Hsp90 species (Figure 1B).
The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).
81.Rationale: The passage indicates that the FP assay for Brugia Hsp90 has high affinity and a large assay window, with no mention of significant background fluorescence compromising detection. For C. elegans extracts, low affinity and potential non-specific binding are noted, but this does not apply to Brugia. The assay procedures described involve standard normalization, not complex ones necessitated by high background.

81.Option: The FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors of Brugia Hsp90 because the structural similarity between Brugia and human Hsp90 prevents meaningful discrimination between the two species.
Evidence: a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C).

The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.
81.Rationale: The experimental data show that the FP assay can differentiate between Brugia and human Hsp90, as evidenced by varying selectivity ratios for different compounds. This indicates that meaningful discrimination is possible, contrary to the option's claim.

81.Option: The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
Evidence: Here we investigated whether the FP assay could be applied to a more complex, multicellular species such as a nematode worm. To investigate this possibility, we prepared extracts from B. pahangi and from the GA-insensitive nematode C. elegans, and probed the affinity of the fluorescent GA molecule, cy3B-GA, for the Hsp90 species characteristic of these two nematodes (Figure 1).
Rewardingly, cy3B-GA bound to the Brugia Hsp90 species (Figure 1A) with a high affinity, similar to the tumor Hsp90 species (Figure 1B).
unlabelled GA displaced cy3B-GA binding to Brugia extracts in a dose-dependent manner, resulting in near 100% displacement at 250–500 nM (Figure 3A).

To evaluate whether the assay is sufficiently sensitive to differentiate between human and Brugia Hsp90 species, and thus lead to the identification of species-selective inhibitors, we measured the affinity of GA and of several closely related PU-class derivatives, PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10 for the Brugia (Figure 4A) and the SKBr3 (Figure 4B) Hsp90 complexes.
81.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the FP assay, originally developed for tumor cells, works effectively for Brugia Hsp90, showing high-affinity binding and dose-dependent competition by inhibitors. Additionally, the assay demonstrates sensitivity in distinguishing between Brugia and human Hsp90, supporting its utility in drug discovery for parasitic nematodes.

81.Option: The high-throughput screening capability of the FP assay is limited by the need for purified recombinant Hsp90 protein, which increases cost and complexity in drug discovery efforts for parasitic nematodes.
Evidence: The assay is suitable for high-throughput screening, does not require the production of purified recombinant protein and could be adapted to screen Hsp90 inhibitors against other parasites, where Hsp90 is known to be a valid target [24], [25].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the assay does not require purified recombinant protein, using worm extracts instead, and is suitable for high-throughput screening. This directly contradicts the option's claim that it is limited by such a requirement.

82.Option: The presence of TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles from multiple T. cruzi strains suggests that this secretion pattern is a conserved virulence strategy across all parasite lineages.
Evidence: Overall, TcTASV-C was secreted in EVs in all the strains analyzed (Fig 4).
these findings also outpoint towards TcTASVs as potential virulence factors and as interesting targets for study and rational intervention.
82.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that TcTASV-C secretion into extracellular vesicles occurs in multiple T. cruzi strains representing different lineages (TcI, TcII, TcVI), indicating conservation of this trait. Furthermore, the passage explicitly suggests that TcTASV proteins are potential virulence factors. Therefore, the conserved secretion pattern can be inferred to be a virulence strategy across lineages.

82.Option: The differential expression of TcTASV-C between bloodstream and culture-derived trypomastigotes indicates that in vitro culture conditions fail to replicate the host immune pressure necessary for proper antigen expression.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel).
82.Rationale: While the passage clearly shows differential expression of TcTASV-C (upregulated in bloodstream trypomastigotes compared to culture-derived forms), it does not provide any evidence regarding the cause of this differential expression. The inference that host immune pressure is the specific factor lacking in culture conditions is not supported by the passage; other environmental factors could be responsible.

82.Option: The secretion of TcTASV-C into extracellular vesicles is a parasite-driven immune evasion mechanism that helps conceal the protein from host immune surveillance.
Evidence: The delivery of molecules in vesicles is a well known immune evasion mechanism exploited by parasites [27]. We therefore investigated whether infected hosts could recognize the protein content of secreted vesicles (Fig 7).

Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with 'naked' secreted proteins (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
82.Rationale: The passage directly links extracellular vesicle secretion to immune evasion, noting that chronically infected hosts' sera fail to recognize antigens within EVs while reacting strongly to the same antigens in other forms. This indicates that packaging in EVs conceals TcTASV-C from immune detection, supporting the inference that it is an immune evasion strategy.

82.Option: The upregulation of TcTASV-C in bloodstream trypomastigotes compared to culture-derived forms suggests that the protein plays a critical role in parasite survival within the host environment.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel).
This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.
these findings also outpoint towards TcTASVs as potential virulence factors and as interesting targets for study and rational intervention.
82.Rationale: The marked upregulation of TcTASV-C specifically in bloodstream trypomastigotes (the stage within the host) implies that the protein is important for parasite functions in the host environment. The passage further suggests that TcTASV proteins are potential virulence factors, supporting the inference that TcTASV-C plays a critical role in parasite survival or virulence within the host.

82.Option: The failure of chronically infected hosts to recognize TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles suggests that the protein is degraded before reaching the immune system.
Evidence: Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with 'naked' secreted proteins (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
82.Rationale: The passage shows that chronically infected hosts' sera fail to recognize TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles but do react with the same protein in trypomastigote lysates and the vesicle-free fraction. This indicates that the protein is not degraded (since it is detectable in other forms) but rather concealed within EVs, as suggested by the immune evasion mechanism discussed. Therefore, the inference of degradation is not supported.

83.Option: Exposure to sand fly saliva in individuals with filarial infection leads to a shift toward a Th1-dominated immune response, enhancing protection against Leishmania.
Evidence: monocytes of SF+/MF+ individuals showed a significantly higher expression of CCL22, a type-2 chemokine (Fig 4), suggesting that exposure to sand fly saliva does not change the MF-induced type-2 phenotype.
disease exacerbation is correlated with the induction of Th2 and/or regulatory cytokines [19].
83.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that exposure to sand fly saliva in filarial-infected individuals leads to a Th1-dominated response. Instead, results show increased expression of CCL22 (a type-2 chemokine) in SF+/MF+ individuals, and the background indicates that disease exacerbation is associated with Th2/regulatory responses. Thus, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

83.Option: Repeated exposure to sand fly saliva renders human monocytes unresponsive to subsequent antigenic stimulation, including with filarial and leishmanial antigens or LPS.
Evidence: stimulation with SG Pd did not induce any of the proinflammatory, type-2 or regulatory cytokines in monocytes of both groups despite having originated from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites (Fig 3).
exposure to sand fly bites also dampened the monocyte response to MF antigen and even to LPS, observed for some cytokines (Fig 3).
83.Rationale: The passage directly states that monocytes from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites show no cytokine induction upon stimulation with sand fly saliva, and their responses to other antigens like MF antigen and LPS are also dampened. This supports the inference that repeated saliva exposure renders monocytes unresponsive to various immune stimuli.

83.Option: The heightened baseline cytokine production in monocytes from Malian individuals may be due to chronic exposure to sand fly bites, leading to a state of persistent immune activation.
Evidence: This could be due to a much higher basal cytokine expression in monocytes of individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bite (Fig 3; closed circles) than unexposed individuals from North America (Fig 1; closed circles).
83.Rationale: The passage explicitly suggests that the higher basal cytokine expression in monocytes from Malian individuals, compared to North American donors, may be attributed to chronic exposure to sand fly bites, implying a state of persistent immune activation. This is a direct inference from the evidence presented.

83.Option: The dampened monocyte response to LPS in SF+ individuals is primarily due to the presence of microfilariae, rather than prior exposure to sand fly saliva.
Evidence: exposure to sand fly bites also dampened the monocyte response to MF antigen and even to LPS, observed for some cytokines (Fig 3).
monocytes of MF+ individuals were less responsive than monocytes of MF- individuals since LPS significantly induced the production of CCL4 and IL-10 only in MF- individuals (Fig 3).
83.Rationale: The passage indicates that exposure to sand fly bites itself dampens monocyte responses, including to LPS, and that microfilariae infection further reduces responsiveness. Since both SF+ groups have prior saliva exposure, the dampening cannot be attributed primarily to microfilariae alone; sand fly exposure also plays a role. Thus, this inference is not supported by the evidence.

83.Option: The upregulation of CCL22 and increased frequency of CCL22+ monocytes in SF+/MF+ individuals is a direct result of repeated exposure to sand fly saliva.
Evidence: monocytes of SF+/MF+ individuals showed a significantly higher expression of CCL22, a type-2 chemokine (Fig 4), suggesting that exposure to sand fly saliva does not change the MF-induced type-2 phenotype.
These data suggest that exposure to sand fly saliva through bites doesn’t change the regulatory phenotype that has already been established by helminth infection.
83.Rationale: The passage shows that both SF+ groups are exposed to sand fly saliva, but only SF+/MF+ individuals have higher CCL22 expression. The text suggests this is due to the filarial infection (MF-induced), not directly from sand fly saliva exposure. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the evidence.

84.Option: The lower seroconversion rates in Bolivia may be attributed to the use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in the Sucre program, which have lower sensitivity and may have led to misclassification of true positive cases.
Evidence: In Sucre, screening was conducting using Chagas Stat-Pak on whole blood. As in Entre Ríos, positive results were confirmed using conventional ELISA and HAI, with tiebreakers assessed via recombinant ELISA.
84.Rationale: The passage indicates that RDT-positive results were systematically confirmed with conventional ELISA and HAI, making misclassification due to lower sensitivity unlikely. There is no evidence in the passage that RDT sensitivity affected seroconversion rates or that misclassification occurred.

84.Option: The lack of a reliable test for parasitological cure is the main reason for the observed differences in seroconversion rates between Central and South American programs.
Evidence: Based on WHO protocol, cure was defined as two non-reactive ELISA tests (one conventional, one recombinant) performed on the same sample on the same date.
84.Rationale: The passage defines cure using serological tests (ELISA) and applies this definition consistently across programs. There is no mention of unreliable parasitological cure tests or that such lack explains the differences in seroconversion rates.

84.Option: The higher seroconversion rates in Central America compared to Bolivia may be due to more effective vector control measures implemented in the earlier programs, which reduced the time between infection and treatment initiation.
Evidence: Before starting diagnosis and treatment of patients, a prerequisite was that infestation rates of the given community had to be <3% following national protocols.
84.Rationale: While vector control was a prerequisite in all programs, the passage does not compare the effectiveness of vector control measures across programs or link vector control to the time between infection and treatment initiation. No evidence supports that vector control differences explain the seroconversion rate contrasts.

84.Option: The observed differences in seroconversion rates between the Central American and Bolivian programs may be explained by geographic variation in Trypanosoma cruzi parasite lineages, with T. cruzi type I predominating in Central America and type II in South America.
84.Rationale: The passage contains no information about Trypanosoma cruzi parasite lineages (e.g., type I or II) or geographic variation in strains. This explanation relies on external knowledge and cannot be derived from the passage body.

84.Option: The differences in seroconversion rates between regions may be explained by variations in the age distribution of treated patients, with younger children in Central America having higher cure rates and older children in Bolivia being less responsive to treatment.
Evidence: All four programs focused on pediatric and adolescent patients, but with an increase in age group treated over time: Yoro, <12 years old; Olopa and Entre Ríos, <15; and Sucre, <18. The increase in treatment age groups over time and projects reflected MSF's strategy to first diagnose and treat young children and then expand these services to older children and adolescents.

Seroconversion rates were found to be lower in older age groups compared with younger ones in Entre Ríos: 24.2% (16/66) <5 years old; 4.6% (14/303) 5–9 years old; 1.9% (12/638) 10–14 years old, at 18–60 months follow-up.
Seroprevalence by age group was 5.0% in <5 years old; 14.8% in the age group 5–9 years old; 31.0% 10–14; and 51.7% 15–16.
Seroprevalence by age group was 1.9% <5 years old; 4.1% 5–9; 8.6% 10–14; and 14.2% 15–18.
84.Rationale: The passage directly shows that seroconversion rates are higher in younger children and decrease with age. The Central American programs treated younger age groups and had higher seroconversion rates, while the Bolivian programs included older children and had lower seroconversion rates. This variation in age distribution provides a plausible epidemiological explanation for the contrasting treatment outcomes.

85.Option: The rural population exposed to sand flies will increase over time primarily due to the expansion of urban centers into rural areas, driven by climate-induced migration.
Evidence: The rural population (communities <10,000 inhabitants) exposed to sand flies will increase over time in both CC scenarios (Table 4).
85.Rationale: The passage states that rural population exposure to sand flies will increase over time, but it does not attribute this increase to the expansion of urban centers or climate-induced migration. The increase is likely due to changes in sand fly distributions under climate change scenarios, as modeled in the study, but the specific causal mechanism mentioned in the option is not discussed or derivable from the passage.

85.Option: The greatest increase in sand fly species richness is projected to occur in the Yucatan Peninsula, particularly along its western half, in the states of Yucatan and Campeche.
Evidence: Highest species richness diminishes approximately 17% over time in the Yucatan Peninsula, especially along its western half, in the states of Yucatan and Campeche (Figure 1D, Table 2).
85.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that species richness diminishes in the Yucatan Peninsula over time, indicating a decrease, not an increase. Therefore, the option's claim of 'greatest increase' is directly contradicted by the evidence.

85.Option: The HadCM3 climate model predicts greater geographic expansion for all sand fly species compared to the CSIRO model, which suggests that the HadCM3 model is more accurate for projecting future vector distributions in North America.
Evidence: In the CSIRO model Lu. vexator's ENM expanded over time as for all other species, while it contracted using the HadCM3 model.
the HadCM3 model was chosen according to performance in reproducing regional climate for Mexico, Central America and the Caribbean.
85.Rationale: The passage shows that for at least one species (Lu. vexator), the HadCM3 model predicts contraction while CSIRO predicts expansion, contradicting the claim of greater expansion for all species. Additionally, while HadCM3 was selected for regional climate performance, the passage does not state that it is more accurate for projecting vector distributions universally; accuracy discussions are based on model evaluation metrics like AUC, but no direct comparison of model accuracy for all species is provided.

85.Option: Lutzomyia cruciata is the only sand fly species with significant niche identity with Leishmania spp., indicating that it is the primary vector responsible for all human cases of Leishmaniases in Mexico.
Evidence: The only sand fly with significant niche identity with Leishmania spp. was Lu. cruciata (Figure 8 and Table 5).

Other sand fly species, such as Lutzomyia anthophora [8], Lutzomyia cruciata [9], [11], Lutzomyia diabolica [7], Lutzomyia ovallesi [12], Lutzomyia panamensis [9], Lutzomyia shannoni [7], [9], and Lutzomyia ylephiletor [9], [13], however, have been found naturally infected or experimentally infected with Leishmania spp. [11].
85.Rationale: While Lu. cruciata is the only species with significant niche identity based on the ENM analysis, the passage also indicates that multiple sand fly species are incriminated as vectors through natural or experimental infection. Niche identity suggests distributional overlap, but it does not imply that Lu. cruciata is the primary vector for all human cases; the passage does not provide evidence to support such a broad causal claim about all cases in Mexico.

85.Option: The projected expansion of the ecological niche breadth and centroid shifts of potential vector species could contribute to an increase in parasite dispersal and hence an increase in human transmission hazard.
Evidence: species' geographic range shifts are predicted in the forthcoming decades, as a result of the accelerated rate of climate change which reduces niche suitability in current locations, while offering new suitable colonization sites.

The change of at least two important attributes of a species' ecological niche, the niche breadth (the expressed geographic coverage of the abiotic niche related to the available geographic space) and the niche's geographic centroid (the geometric central point of the specie's geographic range which indicates the latitudinal mid-point of the range) would dramatically affect the geographic epidemiology of Leishmaniases in North America (i.e. the emergence of new regions where transmission cycles could be established due to the convergence of mammal hosts, parasite and vectors, and human population exposed to these vectors).
In both models and CC scenarios (B2 and A2), niche breadth increased over time for all but one species (Figure 6).

There is a direction shift in ENM centroids for all species in all time periods and both CC scenarios; the majority of species shifted to the northwest (64.3%), followed by northeast (35.1%), and minimally to the southwest (0.6%).
85.Rationale: The passage directly links changes in niche breadth and centroid shifts to potential impacts on the epidemiology of leishmaniases, including the emergence of new transmission areas. The expansion of niche breadth and shifts in centroids under climate change scenarios are documented, and the passage explains that these changes could lead to increased human exposure and establishment of transmission cycles. This logically supports the inference that such distributional changes could increase parasite dispersal and human transmission hazard, aligning with epidemiological principles on vector-borne disease spread.

86.Option: The seasonal clustering of melioidosis cases is due to increased rainfall alone, as heavy rainfalls directly promote the growth and spread of B. pseudomallei in soil and water, leading to higher infection rates.
Evidence: Melioidosis cases usually cluster during the rainy season, particularly after the appearance of a heavy rainfall, cyclone or typhoon [6–9].
Flooding enriches the nutrients in the soil, and the pathogen consequently grows and moves from contaminated sites to remote areas via water [10–11].
According to epidemiological evidence, pulmonary melioidosis can be acquired by inhalation after heavy rainfalls and strong winds [6,11].
The incidence of melioidosis (over 70% manifested with pulmonary melioidosis) was associated with the appearance of heavy rainfalls and strong winds such as typhoons [11].
aerosols containing B. pseudomallei-specific DNA appeared during the typhoon season but not during the dry season (in which no typhoon occurred) [24].
The number of melioidosis cases was associated with contaminated aerosol inhalation during typhoon seasons due to pathogen-carrying winds.

The B. pseudomallei-specific DNA concentration in aerosols was positively correlated with the wind direction (300°-360°, northwesterly to northern wind) (p<0.05) and wind speed of the northwesterly wind (p<0.05) in site G.
86.Rationale: While the passage associates rainfall with melioidosis clustering, it integrates multiple factors, particularly wind and aerosols. The evidence shows that heavy rainfalls are accompanied by strong winds during typhoons, and contaminated aerosols carried by these winds are linked to disease incidence. The option incorrectly isolates rainfall as the sole cause, ignoring the critical role of wind-driven aerosol transmission demonstrated in the integrated data.

86.Option: The melioidosis incidence in the endemic area is primarily driven by direct contact with contaminated soil during agricultural activities, as farmers and outdoor workers are at higher risk due to occupational exposure.
Evidence: Subcutaneous inoculation is recognized as a major transmission mode because farmers have an occupational risk of melioidosis [16].
The incidence of melioidosis (over 70% manifested with pulmonary melioidosis) was associated with the appearance of heavy rainfalls and strong winds such as typhoons [11].
we hypothesized that the contaminated aerosols were carried by northwest winds from cropped fields contaminated with B. pseudomallei and enveloped the endemic area during the typhoon season.
The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.

To test this hypothesis, we integrated relevant data, which showed that B. pseudomallei was geographically distributed in northwestern cropped soil, that aerosols contaminated with B. pseudomallei-specific DNA were increased during the typhoon season, that the DNA concentration was related to the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind and that aerosol isolates were genetically identical to human isolates.
86.Rationale: The passage acknowledges direct contact as a transmission mode but emphasizes aerosol inhalation as a key route in this endemic area, especially for pulmonary melioidosis. The integrated molecular, environmental, and climatic data primarily support wind-borne aerosol transmission, not direct contact. Thus, this option cannot be derived as the primary driver from the passage body.

86.Option: The genetic similarity between human and environmental B. pseudomallei isolates indicates that melioidosis is transmitted through ingestion of contaminated drinking water, as previous outbreaks have been linked to waterborne transmission.
Evidence: In humans, a melioidosis outbreak was previously linked to drinking water contaminated with B. pseudomallei [15].
The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.

To test this hypothesis, we integrated relevant data, which showed that B. pseudomallei was geographically distributed in northwestern cropped soil, that aerosols contaminated with B. pseudomallei-specific DNA were increased during the typhoon season, that the DNA concentration was related to the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind and that aerosol isolates were genetically identical to human isolates.
86.Rationale: The passage mentions waterborne transmission as a general possibility, but the genetic similarity in this study is used to link soil isolates to aerosols and human cases, supporting aerosol inhalation. The integrated data focuses on wind-driven aerosol transmission, not water ingestion. Therefore, this inference cannot be drawn from the passage body.

86.Option: The melioidosis transmission mode in this endemic area includes environmental aerosol inhalation, as evidenced by the molecular match between B. pseudomallei isolates from contaminated soil, aerosols, and human patients, along with the correlation between aerosol DNA concentration and northwesterly winds during the typhoon season.
Evidence: we hypothesized that the contaminated aerosols were carried by northwest winds from cropped fields contaminated with B. pseudomallei and enveloped the endemic area during the typhoon season.

To test this hypothesis, we integrated relevant data, which showed that B. pseudomallei was geographically distributed in northwestern cropped soil, that aerosols contaminated with B. pseudomallei-specific DNA were increased during the typhoon season, that the DNA concentration was related to the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind and that aerosol isolates were genetically identical to human isolates.
