264.Option: The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants [29] who instead of being connected by rivers, live in a human-populated, dry forest landscape connected by roads, and watered by small streams feeding into the Pedro Pablo Gómez and Grande Rivers (Figs 1 and 5); in this area, use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.

Thus, the option includes details not present in the passage and cannot be derived from it.

264.Option: The high prevalence of small trematode eggs in the human population of the three communities reported here may still be an underestimate considering the low sensitivity of small trematode egg detection using the formalin-ether concentration technique and one-time fecal sample examination.

While it mentions that molecular and immunological methods could improve diagnosis, this refers to discrimination between species, not necessarily sensitivity of detection. Epidemiological reasoning cannot derive clinical ineffectiveness from in vitro data alone without supporting clinical data.

Evidence: The cluster NE/SD-B1 (n = 10) contained strains of putative hybrid or mixed genotypes

we hypothesised that pre-existing antibodies to O. tsutsugamushi may increase the risk of severe infection.

267.Rationale: The passage shows higher risk in intermediate and high IgG categories, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to a lack of protective immunity. Moreover, high levels also confer increased risk, so the inference is not uniquely supported by the data. Therefore, this conclusion is not directly derivable from the passage.

Therefore, this inference is not directly derivable from the experimental findings presented.

269.Option: The presence of C. burnetii antibodies in asymptomatic companion dogs is of particular importance as they can transmit the disease to humans; thus posing a potential risk of Q fever outbreak.
Evidence: Among dogs, two studies reported on C. burnetii prevalence in Iran, yielding a pooled prevalence of 7.7–11% with tick infested dogs that had been referred to the veterinary teaching hospital of Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman having a higher seroprevalence (11%) compared to the 7.7% prevalence reported among asymptomatic companion dogs in Fars province, in the southern part of Iran.
Transmission to human most frequently occurs through inhalation of aerosolized bacteria that are spread in the environment by infected animals after delivery or abortion.
269.Rationale: The passage reports seroprevalence of C. burnetii in asymptomatic dogs but does not establish that these dogs are significant transmitters to humans. Transmission is primarily through inhalation of aerosols from infected animals like cattle, sheep, and goats, not directly from dogs. There is no evidence in the passage linking asymptomatic dogs to human outbreaks, so this inference cannot be derived.

269.Option: The relatively high C. burnetii seroprevalence reported among goats and sheep in Iran as compared to other animals makes them perfect reservoirs and potential agents of C. burnetii transmission to other animals and humans.

Individual pooled prevalence among sheep was intermediate of the two at 19.5–36.5% [110, 112, 117, 121, 126, 137, 162, 163].
The main reservoirs of C. burnetii are cattle, sheep, and goats.
269.Rationale: The passage shows higher seroprevalence in goats and sheep compared to cattle, and identifies them as main reservoirs. This supports the inference that they are important for transmission to other animals and humans, as they can harbor and shed the pathogen.

on the other hand, molecular techniques (PCR) were utilized for most (86.7%) anaplasmosis studies
269.Rationale: The passage highlights that molecular techniques improve accurate identification and are already used in many studies. 269.Option: The existence of C. burnetii, Ehrlichia spp., Anaplasma spp. and Rickettia rickettsii in a number of organisms in 22 out of the 31 provinces in Iran as reported by various studies implies that the diseases caused by these pathogens could be highly prevalent in the country.
Evidence: Collectively, the different studies were reported in 22 (70.9%) of the 31 provinces of Iran
the presence of a number of Ixodid ticks in Iran therefore implies that there could be many natural foci of tickborne zoonoses in the country
Overwhelming evidence shows that tick borne pathogens in the Order Rickettsiales and Legionellales are prevalent in the Middle East region including Iran.
269.Rationale: Studies have detected multiple pathogens in organisms across most provinces, indicating widespread presence. This, combined with the existence of tick vectors, supports the inference that these diseases could be highly prevalent, even though data might be incomplete.

269.Option: The geographic clustering of studies in the northern and southern parts of Iran suggests that tick-borne zoonoses are primarily confined to these regions and unlikely to spread to central or eastern provinces.
Evidence: Geographically, a large proportion of studies on coxiellosis were distributed in provinces located to the southern (46%) and northern (41.8%) parts of the county
most studies on anaplasmosis (64.3%) and ehrlichiosis (58.3%) were concentrated to the northern part of the country

the presence of a number of Ixodid ticks in Iran therefore implies that there could be many natural foci of tickborne zoonoses in the country from which they may spread to other areas under changing socio-economic and climatic conditions.
269.Rationale: While studies are clustered in northern and southern regions, the passage explicitly states that tick-borne diseases could spread to other areas due to changing conditions. The clustering reflects study distribution bias, not confinement of diseases, so this inference contradicts the passage.

270.Option: The dual-coding nature of certain mitochondrial genes in T. brucei may protect essential electron transport chain genes from genetic drift during periods of relaxed selection in the mammalian host.
Evidence: when the mitochondrial genes are unused, they are not under selection, hence the integrity of these genes are threatened by genetic drift

Exacerbating this, salivarian trypanosomes undergo a severe bottleneck as they transition through the tsetse fly and into the mammalian host, and then within the bloodstream, they undergo multiple bottlenecks at each antigenic switch, as they evade the host immune system. Such bottlenecks create additional forces of genetic drift, where genes can be lost even if their deleterious fitness effect is considerable.
We hypothesize that trypanosomes use dual-coding genes to protect genetic information by essentially hiding a non-selected (ETC) gene within one that remains under selection.
270.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes how mitochondrial genes, especially those for the electron transport chain (ETC), are not under selection during the mammalian host phase when the mitochondrion is down-regulated, making them vulnerable to genetic drift exacerbated by bottlenecks. The authors hypothesize that dual-coding genes protect this genetic information by hiding non-selected ETC genes within genes that remain under selection. This directly supports the evolutionary advantage of protection from genetic drift.

270.Option: The presence of alternative editing events that generate functionally distinct protein isoforms suggests that RNA editing in trypanosomes evolved primarily to increase proteomic diversity in the bloodstream form.
Evidence: Alternative editing of Cytochrome Oxidase III (COIII) is reported to generate a novel DNA-binding protein, AEP-1, that functions in mitochondrial DNA maintenance
Here, we show that as many as six additional cryptogenes also encode for more than one protein.

Instead, the passage focuses on the question of why the fragile and expensive editing system persists and hypothesizes that dual-coding is a gene-protective strategy. The primary evolutionary advantage discussed is protection from genetic drift, not increasing proteomic diversity.

270.Option: The dual-coding structure of mitochondrial genes in salivarian trypanosomes allows for the simultaneous expression of both the canonical ETC protein and a novel transporter protein in the bloodstream form, enhancing metabolic efficiency.
Evidence: During this stage of the life cycle, the mitochondrion is down-regulated, lacking both Krebs cycle enzymes and a functional electron transport chain (ETC)
We hypothesize that trypanosomes use dual-coding genes to protect genetic information by essentially hiding a non-selected (ETC) gene within one that remains under selection.

The dual-coding structure is hypothesized to protect genes from drift, not to enable simultaneous expression for metabolic efficiency. There is no evidence that both proteins are expressed simultaneously in the bloodstream form or that this enhances metabolic efficiency.

270.Option: The high number of gRNA variants observed in the terminal gRNA populations of pan-edited genes indicates that trypanosomes use RNA editing to regulate gene expression through stochastic alternative splicing-like mechanisms.
Evidence: analyses of the gRNA transcriptome for the pan-edited transcripts indicate that full editing involves a large number of gRNA populations
most of the gRNA populations (population defined as guiding the same or near same region of the mRNA) contain multiple sequence classes.

The evolutionary advantage discussed is gene protection via dual-coding, not regulation of expression. No evidence is provided for stochastic or splicing-like mechanisms.

270.Option: The extensive RNA editing process in trypanosomes evolved to compensate for the high mutation rate in the mitochondrial genome by enabling the repair of frameshifts through gRNA-directed U-insertions and deletions.
Evidence: RNA editing creates open reading frames in “cryptogenes” by insertion and deletion of uridylate residues at specific sites within the mRNA
editing proceeds from the 3’ end to the 5’ end of the mRNA transcript with the terminal gRNA (last one in the cascade) often creating the start codon needed for translation
270.Rationale: While RNA editing does involve repair of frameshifts and creation of ORFs, the passage does not state that it evolved to compensate for high mutation rates. Instead, it emphasizes genetic drift as the threat due to relaxed selection and bottlenecks. The hypothesis focuses on dual-coding as a protective strategy against drift, not mutation repair as the primary evolutionary advantage.

271.Option: The declining trend in CHIKV seroprevalence from 2000–2009 to 2010–2019 suggests that control measures have been effective in reducing transmission.
Evidence: Interestingly, there was a declining trend of CHIKV seropositivity over time, as CHIKV seroprevalence was significantly lower during the period 2010–2019 (18%, 95% CI: 14–22) than 2000–2009 (35%, 95% CI: 27–43).
271.Rationale: The passage reports a declining trend in CHIKV seroprevalence over time but does not provide any explanation or causal attribution. It does not mention control measures or their effectiveness. Therefore, the inference that control measures have been effective is not supported by the passage and requires external assumptions.

271.Option: The high proportions of inapparent infections in our study indicate that efforts should be scaled up to develop a more effective surveillance and monitoring system.
Evidence: Approximately 80% of individuals infected by DENV and ZIKV remain asymptomatic throughout the infection, presenting no symptoms or clinical signs of infection [4,19–22], which lead to under-estimation and under-reporting by official passive surveillance and reporting systems.
Seroprevalence studies, as a supplement for traditional symptom-based and laboratory-based surveillance, are essential for assessing the true disease burden of mosquito-borne diseases.
Globally, the average proportion of inapparent/total DENV infections was 80% (95% CI: 72–88)
the average proportion of inapparent CHIKV infections to all CHIKV infections was 40% (95% CI: 24–56)
the average proportion of inapparent ZIKV infections to all ZIKV infections was 50% (95% CI: 18–82)
271.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that high proportions of infections are asymptomatic, leading to underestimation and under-reporting by current surveillance systems. It emphasizes that seroprevalence studies are essential to assess true burden. This directly supports the inference that more effective surveillance and monitoring systems are needed to capture the true impact of these arboviruses.

271.Option: A considerable proportion of cases are undiagnosed or unreported and the true burden of these arbovirus infections is largely unknown, which reinforces the need for population screening especially in epidemic areas.
Evidence: The disparity between the number of reported cases and estimates of actual cases makes it tough to capture the true burden of these mosquito-borne diseases.

Approximately 80% of individuals infected by DENV and ZIKV remain asymptomatic throughout the infection, presenting no symptoms or clinical signs of infection [4,19–22], which lead to under-estimation and under-reporting by official passive surveillance and reporting systems.
Seroprevalence studies, as a supplement for traditional symptom-based and laboratory-based surveillance, are essential for assessing the true disease burden of mosquito-borne diseases.
271.Rationale: The passage directly highlights the challenge of under-reporting due to asymptomatic infections and the resulting difficulty in capturing true burden. It states that seroprevalence studies are essential, which inherently involves population screening. While the phrase 'especially in epidemic areas' is not explicitly stated, the need for such screening to understand true burden is a logical extension of the passage's emphasis on seroprevalence studies in endemic and affected regions.

271.Option: The seroprevalence of DENV and CHIKV in developing countries was higher than in developed countries, indicating a direct link between socioeconomic status and transmission intensity.
Evidence: DENV seroprevalences were not significantly different in developed (30%, 95% CI: 15–46) versus developing (38%, 95% CI: 31–44) countries.
Subgroup analysis ... showed no significant differences in seroprevalences of CHIKV between developed (14% 95% CI: 6–22) and developing (26%, 95% CI: 22–30) countries.
271.Rationale: The passage reports point estimates that are higher in developing countries but explicitly states there were no significant differences based on overlapping confidence intervals. Moreover, it does not discuss any causal link between socioeconomic status and transmission intensity; it merely reports seroprevalence estimates without inferring causation. Therefore, the inference of a direct link is not supported by the evidence presented.

271.Option: The use of ELISA IgG as the primary diagnostic method may lead to overestimation of seroprevalence due to cross-reactivity with other arboviruses.
Evidence: There were a variety of assays used for serological diagnosis across studies. The most commonly used assay was enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA; 89.5%, 119/133), followed by neutralization assay (NA; 6.0%, 8/133) and immunofluorescence assay (IFA; 3.8%, 5/133).
271.Rationale: The passage notes that ELISA was the most commonly used assay but does not discuss its limitations, such as cross-reactivity with other arboviruses. No evidence is provided to suggest that ELISA may lead to overestimation of seroprevalence. This inference would require external knowledge about serological assay performance, which is not addressed in the passage.

272.Option: The media coverage of diseases in Espírito Santo is driven more by emotional appeal, newsworthiness criteria, and economic interests than by epidemiological relevance, particularly for diseases affecting disadvantaged populations.
Evidence: The reasons highlighted for media coverage (or not) of certain diseases included the political and economic interests of the newspapers, their editorial line of work, and the organizational routine of the newsrooms.
the alleged public interest was highlighted, considering the common sense of citizens (such as aesthetic issues in health) and the emotional appeal that certain diseases have
The parameter [to cover the news] is: is the news recent? Is the news appealing? Will it reach many people?

In principle, news is that which interests most people; it has to cause an impact, right […] it needs to be of interest to the reader, especially if it’s health news, it has to contain information that will interest as many people as possible.
Emphasis was placed on poverty-related diseases, including tuberculosis, leprosy, schistosomiasis, leishmaniasis, and trachoma.
We believe in the political importance of the identification of DNMs, which are considered journalistically uninteresting because they do not meet the usual criteria of newsworthiness.
272.Rationale: The passage provides multiple pieces of evidence that journalists prioritize newsworthiness (recency, appeal, reach), emotional appeal, and economic interests when deciding which health topics to cover. The identification of Diseases Neglected by the Media (DNMs)—including poverty-related diseases that affect disadvantaged populations—despite their high 'health value' (epidemiological relevance) directly demonstrates that coverage is not primarily driven by epidemiological importance but by these other factors.

272.Option: The media coverage of diseases in Espírito Santo is primarily guided by the health value of diseases as determined by epidemiological indicators such as mortality, morbidity, and hospitalization rates.
Evidence: To identify the most epidemiologically important health problems, we analysed data on health policies and morbidity and mortality in Espírito Santo between 2011 and 2012 and developed a composite indicator designated 'health value'.
DNMs were quantitatively identified by evaluating the prioritized diseases that were not covered in the print media.
272.Rationale: While the study constructs a 'health value' indicator based on epidemiological and policy priorities to assess disease importance, the very identification of Diseases Neglected by the Media (DNMs) shows that many conditions with high health value receive limited coverage. Journalists' perspectives further indicate that coverage decisions are based on other criteria (e.g., newsworthiness, economic interests), not primarily on epidemiological relevance.

272.Option: The media coverage of diseases in Espírito Santo is primarily influenced by the public’s demand for health information, with news content shaped by audience preferences and civic engagement.
Evidence: The reasons highlighted for media coverage (or not) of certain diseases included the political and economic interests of the newspapers, their editorial line of work, and the organizational routine of the newsrooms.
the alleged public interest was highlighted, considering the common sense of citizens (such as aesthetic issues in health) and the emotional appeal that certain diseases have

In principle, news is that which interests most people; it has to cause an impact, right […] it needs to be of interest to the reader, especially if it’s health news, it has to contain information that will interest as many people as possible.
272.Rationale: Although journalists mention 'public interest' as a factor, this is interpreted as what appeals emotionally or reaches many people, not necessarily as a response to public demand for health information or civic engagement. Moreover, the passage highlights that political and economic interests, editorial lines, and organizational routines also shape coverage. The neglect of epidemiologically important diseases suggests that coverage does not primarily reflect audience preferences for comprehensive health information.

272.Option: The media coverage of diseases in Espírito Santo reflects a balanced and objective representation of public health priorities, as confirmed by the alignment between health policy goals and news reporting.
Evidence: Diseases considered relevant by the print media in Espírito Santo were those that reached the health value of the state and were among the most reported diseases
DNMs were quantitatively identified by evaluating the prioritized diseases that were not covered in the print media.
272.Rationale: While some diseases with high health value (e.g., dengue, AIDS) are covered, the study explicitly identifies Diseases Neglected by the Media (DNMs) that are prioritized in health policy but receive limited media attention. This mismatch demonstrates that media coverage does not provide a balanced or objective representation of public health priorities; instead, significant gaps exist.

272.Option: The media coverage of diseases in Espírito Santo is shaped primarily by the availability of expert sources and the professional expertise of journalists, which ensures accurate and comprehensive reporting on public health issues.
Evidence: The reasons highlighted for media coverage (or not) of certain diseases included the political and economic interests of the newspapers, their editorial line of work, and the organizational routine of the newsrooms.
DNMs were quantitatively identified by evaluating the prioritized diseases that were not covered in the print media.
272.Rationale: The passage does not mention expert sources or journalist expertise as factors influencing coverage. Instead, journalists cite political and economic interests, editorial lines, and organizational routines. The existence of DNMs—diseases of high epidemiological importance that are under-covered—directly contradicts the claim that reporting is accurate and comprehensive.

273.Option: The stage-specific effects of CsA are governed by independent mechanisms linked to inhibition of calcineurin phosphatase activity in promastigotes, and inhibition of CyP functions relevant for thermotolerance in amastigotes.
Evidence: CsA exerts its inhibitory action through binding of CyPs and inactivation of the cellular phosphatase calcineurin by CsA/CyP complexes
FK506 treatment of promastigotes induced morphological changes similar to CsA treated parasites, and strongly reduced in vitro growth and cell proliferation in a dose-dependent manner
FK506 treatment of axenic amastigotes did not reproduce the CsA effects.
unlike CsA, FK506 did not induce massive cell death in amastigotes even at the highest concentration
CsA treatment reduces L. donovani thermotolerance
CsA affects thermotolerance of both the promastigote and amastigote stages
Leishmania CyPs may have important amastigote-specific chaperone functions and participate in protein disaggregation
CsA-mediated amastigote killing is temperature dependent.
273.Rationale: The passage shows that for promastigotes, both CsA and FK506 (another calcineurin inhibitor) cause similar growth inhibition and morphological changes, suggesting that calcineurin inhibition is a key mechanism. For amastigotes, CsA induces cell death and reduces thermotolerance, effects not observed with FK506, indicating a different mechanism. The passage links CyPs to chaperone functions and thermotolerance, and demonstrates CsA binding to CyP40 and inhibition of its PPIase activity. This supports the inference that stage-specific effects involve independent mechanisms: calcineurin inhibition in promastigotes and inhibition of CyP functions relevant for thermotolerance in amastigotes.

273.Option: The temperature-dependent toxicity of CsA in Leishmania is due to increased drug uptake at higher temperatures, leading to enhanced inhibition of calcineurin in both promastigotes and amastigotes.
Evidence: CsA-treatment of amastigotes at 26°C completely abrogated the toxic effect of the inhibitor.
CsA affects thermotolerance of both the promastigote and amastigote stages
273.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that CsA toxicity is temperature-dependent, but it does not provide evidence that this is due to increased drug uptake at higher temperatures. Instead, the passage links the temperature-dependent effects to CsA's impact on CyP functions critical for thermotolerance. The differential effects of CsA and FK506 on amastigotes suggest that calcineurin inhibition may not be the primary mechanism for amastigote killing, and no data support enhanced calcineurin inhibition due to temperature-mediated drug uptake.

273.Option: CsA treatment of promastigotes induces morphological changes resembling amastigotes without inducing the appropriate gene expression profile, suggesting that CsA triggers a partial differentiation program.
Evidence: CsA-treatment of promastigotes induced morphological changes reminiscent of axenic amastigotes, including increased aggregate formation, oval cell shape, and shortened and retracted flagella

Despite the amastigote-like morphology, CsA-treated promastigotes maintain expression of LPG, comparable to the level of solvent treated cells promastigotes, and do not show induction of the amastigote marker protein A2
273.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CsA treatment causes promastigotes to adopt morphological features similar to amastigotes, but without changing the expression of stage-specific markers (LPG remains high, A2 is not induced). This discrepancy between morphology and gene expression implies an incomplete differentiation process, supporting the inference that CsA triggers a partial differentiation program.

274.Option: The increased constitutive phosphorylation of STATs in patients with advanced cardiomyopathy compensates for the signal transduction defects found in these subjects, as evidenced by the decreased gene expression of downstream STAT genes coding for T-bet (TBX21) and eomesodermin (EOMES).
Evidence: Low levels of STAT phosphorylation in response to IL-27 in subjects with heart disease were associated with increased constitutive STAT phosphorylation (Fig 3) and increased constitutive gene expression of these transcription factors (S4A–S4C Fig).

In response to IL-27, gene expression of TBX21, EOMES, and CXCL9, which are activated downstream of STAT1, STAT3, and STAT5 phosphorylation, was lower in patients with cardiomyopathy (i.e., the G1 and G2 groups) than that in uninfected subjects (S4D–S4E and S4G Fig).

Overall, downregulation of IL-27R in CD4+ T cells and upregulation in CD8+ T cells reflected a functional IL-27R axis with active STAT phosphorylation after IL-27 stimulation, whereas increased constitutive phosphorylated STAT was associated with unresponsiveness to IL-27.
274.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that increased constitutive STAT phosphorylation in patients with cardiomyopathy is associated with unresponsiveness to IL-27 and decreased downstream gene expression of TBX21 and EOMES. This indicates a defect in signal transduction, not compensation. The evidence suggests that constitutive phosphorylation is linked to impaired signaling rather than compensatory mechanisms.

274.Option: The in vitro assay with IL-2 blocking antibodies indicates that these findings may be explained by the inhibition of IL-27R expression by autocrine IL-2 regulation, a cytokine predominantly produced by CD4+ rather than that by CD8+ T cells upon activation.
Evidence: As IL-2 is predominantly secreted by CD4+ T cells [40] and inhibits the expression of WSX-1 in activated CD4+ T cells in an autocrine manner [29], we also evaluated the effect of neutralizing IL-2 on the IL-27R expression in T cells in in vitro IL-27-stimulation assays.

The downregulation of the IL-27R expression in CD4+ T cells was inhibited (Fig 2A–2C), whereas the upregulation of the IL-27R in CD8+ T cells was not affected by IL-2 neutralization (Fig 2A and 2D–2E).
274.Rationale: The Passage Body directly describes an in vitro assay with IL-2 blocking antibodies, which demonstrates that autocrine IL-2 regulation inhibits IL-27R expression in CD4+ T cells. This is a mechanistic finding that explains part of the observed immune signaling dysfunction and is derivable from the experimental results.

274.Option: The impairment of the IL-27R signaling pathway in chronic Chagas disease is associated with disease progression, as evidenced by reduced STAT phosphorylation and diminished polyfunctional T-cell responses in patients with cardiomyopathy.
Evidence: In contrast, in patients with heart disease (i.e., G1, G2/G3 groups), STAT phosphorylation did not significantly increase after IL-27 stimulation.
The number of subjects with augmented STAT phosphorylation in response to IL-27 was significantly lower in the G2/G3 subject group than those in the uninfected group (S3 Fig).

In contrast to that observed in subjects without cardiomyopathy, the addition of IL-27 or IL-7 in T. cruzi-stimulated cell cultures of PBMCs derived from those with heart disease (i.e., the G2 group) did not improve the frequencies of monofunctional or polyfunctional CD4+ T-cell responses specific for T. cruzi.
The impairment of T. cruzi-specific T-cell function is more pronounced in patients with advanced cardiomyopathy [6,8,10].
274.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence that patients with cardiomyopathy (more severe clinical forms) have reduced STAT phosphorylation in response to IL-27 and diminished polyfunctional T-cell responses compared to those without heart disease. This indicates an association between impaired IL-27R signaling and disease progression in chronic Chagas disease.

275.Option: The absence of leishmanolysin in amastigotes leads to a general increase in susceptibility to all BMAP-28 isoforms, with the D-isoform showing the greatest relative improvement.
275.Rationale: The passage does not provide data demonstrating a general increase in susceptibility for all BMAP-28 isoforms in the absence of leishmanolysin for amastigotes. While the D-isoform shows improved efficacy against ko amastigotes, no comparative data is presented for the L-isoform between wild-type and ko strains in the amastigote assay to support a generalized increase.

275.Option: The D-isoform of BMAP-28 is more effective than the L-isoform in reducing amastigote burden because it is resistant to degradation by macrophage enzymes in the lysosomal compartment.
275.Rationale: The passage does not discuss degradation by macrophage enzymes in the lysosomal compartment as a mechanism for the efficacy of the D-isoform. While protease resistance to leishmanolysin is mentioned, this is specific to the parasite's protease and not macrophage enzymes. The inferred causal explanation is not supported by the experimental data.

275.Option: The D-isoform of BMAP-28 is more effective at reducing amastigote burden in macrophages than the L-isoform, particularly in the absence of leishmanolysin.
Evidence: However, the D- form of the peptide was shown to be most effective at reducing the number of L. major ko amastigotes upon treatment with 0.5 µM D-BMAP-28.

This differs dramatically from the wt infection where treatment of 0.5 µM D-BMAP-28 resulted in only 30% inhibition, demonstrating that the D-isoform of BMAP-28 was again more effective against the parasite.
275.Rationale: The experimental data from the amastigote assay directly show that in the absence of leishmanolysin (ko strain), D-BMAP-28 is the most effective isoform at reducing amastigote burden, implying superior efficacy compared to the L-isoform. The comparison is explicit in the context of leishmanolysin absence.

275.Option: The L-isoform of BMAP-28 is more effective than the D-isoform in reducing amastigote burden when leishmanolysin is absent, due to its higher affinity for the parasite membrane.
275.Rationale: The passage contradicts this claim by demonstrating that the D-isoform is more effective in reducing amastigote burden in the absence of leishmanolysin. No evidence is provided regarding higher membrane affinity for the L-isoform; instead, the data support the superiority of the D-isoform under these conditions.

276.Option: S. mansoni infection risk is more strongly associated with water access site features that ensure low flow habitat where Biomphalaria snails can survive than with the total amount of non-emergent vegetation locally available to colonize.
Evidence: water access site shoreline width was negatively correlated with S. mansoni re-infection
circumscription was positively correlated with re-infection in villages located on the lake

For S. mansoni infection outcomes, snail habitat (non-emergent vegetation) was correlated to neither infection presence nor egg burden accumulation in children at any offshore spatial extent (45 m to 120 m sampling radii).

water access site morphology (i.e., site size and shape features) could differentially influence Bulinus and Biomphalaria snail abundance and human infection risk for the two parasites, by mediating water access site flow rates, depth, temperature, vegetation, and snail population connectivity.
276.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that S. mansoni infection risk is significantly associated with water access site features (shoreline width and circumscription), while it shows no association with non-emergent vegetation at offshore radii. Since site morphology mediates flow rates, it can be epidemiologically inferred that features ensuring suitable flow conditions are more strongly associated with infection risk than the total non-emergent vegetation.

276.Option: The positive effect of vegetation on snail abundance at a large radius greatly outweighs the negative effect of vegetation on host-finding behavior close to shorelines, suggesting that non-emergent vegetation is a net driver of S. haematobium transmission risk.
Evidence: Nearshore snail habitat (the amount of non-emergent vegetation within 1 m and 5 m sampling radii) was negatively associated with S. haematobium infection presence in children.
the association was positive when snail habitat was measured within a sampling radius of at least 90 m from shorelines
Bulinus spp. snails were at their highest densities in non-emergent vegetation outside water access sites
276.Rationale: While the passage shows positive associations between snail habitat at large radii and S. haematobium infection risk, and negative associations at nearshore, it does not mention host-finding behavior or provide evidence for why nearshore vegetation is negatively associated. The inference about host-finding behavior outweighing is not derivable solely from the passage; it introduces external concepts not supported by the evidence.

276.Option: The correlation between the local availability of snail habitat within 90 m or more from shorelines and village-level S. haematobium infection presence and egg burden could be driven by two general factors: first, a larger radius results in a larger sampling effort, which likely reduces error in snail habitat assessment, especially where snail habitat is heterogeneous.
276.Rationale: The passage presents correlations between snail habitat at different radii and infection risk but does not discuss sampling effort, error reduction, or heterogeneity in snail habitat assessment. This option is speculative and not supported by any evidence from the passage.

276.Option: For S. haematobium, human infection risk is best correlated with remotely-sensed Bulinus spp. snail habitat measured within a sampling radius of 90 m or more from water access site shorelines, whereas S. mansoni infection risk is not associated with snail habitat at these offshore sampling radii.
Evidence: the association was positive when snail habitat was measured within a sampling radius of at least 90 m from shorelines
AIC was lowest for the model integrating snail habitat at a 90 m sampling radius

For S. mansoni infection outcomes, snail habitat (non-emergent vegetation) was correlated to neither infection presence nor egg burden accumulation in children at any offshore spatial extent (45 m to 120 m sampling radii).
276.Rationale: The evidence directly shows that S. haematobium infection risk and egg burden are positively correlated with snail habitat measured at sampling radii of 90 m or more, with optimal model fit at 90 m. In contrast, S. mansoni infection risk shows no association with snail habitat at any offshore spatial extent. This is a clear epidemiological inference from the study results.

276.Option: In the Senegal River Basin, S. mansoni infection risk is positively correlated with snail habitat measured within a sampling radius of 90 m or more from water access site shorelines, similar to S. haematobium.
Evidence: For S. mansoni infection outcomes, snail habitat (non-emergent vegetation) was correlated to neither infection presence nor egg burden accumulation in children at any offshore spatial extent (45 m to 120 m sampling radii).
276.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that S. mansoni infection risk is not correlated with snail habitat at offshore radii, directly contradicting this option. Therefore, it is incorrect.

277.Option: The very long elimination half-lives observed in some participants at 6 and 12 months suggest that moxidectin may accumulate in the body with repeated dosing, potentially increasing the risk of adverse events in long-term treatment regimens.
277.Rationale: The passage reports the arithmetic mean half-life in days (17.7 to 23.3 days) but does not mention half-lives observed at 6 or 12 months post-dose, nor does it discuss accumulation or increased risk of adverse events from repeated dosing based on pharmacokinetics. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

277.Option: The lack of noteworthy pharmacokinetic drug interactions is another important distinction between moxidectin and ivermectin, suggesting that moxidectin can be safely co-administered with a wide range of medications in onchocerciasis control programs.
277.Rationale: The Passage Body does not mention or provide evidence regarding pharmacokinetic drug interactions for moxidectin or ivermectin. The pharmacokinetic data presented focus on parameters like half-life, clearance, and volume of distribution, with no comparison or discussion of drug interactions. Thus, this inference is not derivable from the passage.

277.Option: Moxidectin’s large volume of distribution and low clearance result in a short duration of action, which limits its utility in mass drug administration programs despite its high lipophilicity.
Evidence: Moxidectin is a low clearance drug with a mean oral clearance (CL/F) ranging from 80.3 to 93.9 L/day and a relatively apparent large volume of distribution (V/F), with mean values ranging from 2222 to 2421 L.
The arithmetic mean T1/2 ranged from 17.7 to 23.3 days.
277.Rationale: The passage indicates that moxidectin has a low clearance and large volume of distribution, which contribute to a long terminal half-life (17.7 to 23.3 days), not a short duration of action. Moreover, the passage highlights moxidectin's prolonged microfilaricidal activity and potential for enhancing elimination, contradicting the claim of limited utility. Therefore, this inference is incorrect based on the evidence.

277.Option: The high-fat breakfast is associated with increased exposure and a delayed time to maximum concentration (Tmax), which may necessitate dietary restrictions in mass drug administration programs to ensure consistent drug absorption.
277.Rationale: The Passage Body states that 'Treatment was taken under observation after an overnight fast' but does not discuss the effect of food, such as a high-fat breakfast, on pharmacokinetic parameters like exposure or Tmax. No information is provided on dietary restrictions or food-drug interactions. Hence, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

277.Option: Moxidectin has a long terminal half-life of approximately 18 to 24 days in O. volvulus infected individuals, which contributes to its potential for sustained antiparasitic activity and supports its use in mass drug administration programs.
Evidence: The arithmetic mean T1/2 ranged from 17.7 to 23.3 days.

Clinical studies in O. volvulus infected individuals completed in Ghana, Liberia and the Democratic Republic of the Congo demonstrated moxidectin’s superiority compared to ivermectin in terms of the extent and duration of reduction of skin microfilariae density, indicating its potential to accelerate and enhance the feasibility of onchocerciasis elimination.
277.Rationale: The passage directly reports a long terminal half-life (17.7 to 23.3 days, approximately 18 to 24 days) for moxidectin in infected individuals. It also links moxidectin's prolonged activity to its superiority in reducing microfilariae density over time, supporting its clinical utility for sustained antiparasitic effects. This aligns with the inference that the pharmacokinetic profile supports use in mass drug administration for disease control.

278.Option: The lack of neutrophilic infiltrates in antibiotic-treated patients indicates that mycolactone-mediated cytotoxicity prevents the recruitment of neutrophils during early infection.
Evidence: mycolactone has cytopathic and apoptotic activity and is thought to be responsible for local immunosuppression by destroying infiltrating cells
Lack of a marked inflammatory response and abundant clusters of extracellular bacilli in one of five antibiotic treated patients
only minor superficial vasculitis-associated infiltrates of the dermis consisting of lymphocytes and macrophages/monocytes were found
In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed
sparsely distributed elastase+ PMNL (Figure 7A) with sporadic focal clusters near ulcerative and necrotic areas
278.Rationale: The passage describes that one non-responding patient had minimal inflammatory infiltrates, while four responding patients had massive cellular infiltration including neutrophils (elastase+ PMNL). Mycolactone is mentioned as causing cytotoxicity, but the passage does not link it specifically to preventing neutrophil recruitment during early infection; it focuses on histopathological findings after antibiotic treatment. The evidence shows neutrophils are present in responders, and the non-responder's lack of infiltrates is not attributed to mycolactone's effect on early recruitment. The inference requires assumptions about early infection not supported by the passage.

278.Option: The presence of extracellular mycobacterial clusters in the lesion of one antibiotic-treated patient suggests that antibiotic treatment may accelerate the progression of early plaques and oedemas to ulcerative lesions.
Evidence: Lack of a marked inflammatory response and abundant clusters of extracellular bacilli in one of five antibiotic treated patients
features of this lesion resembled that of specimens from untreated patients
Although no further ulcer enlargement and reduction of oedema were observed, the responsible clinicians had decided to treat all five patients surgically
278.Rationale: The passage describes one patient who did not respond to antibiotic treatment, with lesions resembling untreated BU. There is no evidence that antibiotic treatment accelerated disease progression; rather, it was ineffective for this patient. The clinicians observed no further ulcer enlargement after antibiotic treatment, indicating stabilization, not acceleration. The inference contradicts the passage's description of treatment aiming to reduce recurrence and avoid surgery.

278.Option: A complex and highly organized cellular immune response is crucial for the elimination of a chronic M. ulcerans infection.
Evidence: Highly organized inflammatory responses and intracellular bacterial material in four of five antibiotic-treated patients
In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed
mycobacterial material was predominantly found inside macrophages
Lack of a marked inflammatory response and abundant clusters of extracellular bacilli in one of five antibiotic treated patients
The aim of the present study was to analyse whether the local immunosuppression in Buruli ulcer lesions can be reversed by the combination treatment with rifampicin and streptomycin
278.Rationale: The passage contrasts the non-responding patient, who had minimal immune response and extracellular bacteria, with four responding patients who had organized immune responses (granulomas, diffuse infiltrates) and intracellular bacterial material. This direct comparison suggests that organized cellular immune responses are associated with bacterial internalization and likely elimination. The study's aim to reverse immunosuppression and the findings in responders support that such responses are crucial for effective treatment outcomes.

278.Option: The presence of CD14+ macrophages and dendritic cells in necrotic areas suggests that antigen presentation occurs independently of granuloma formation in antibiotic-treated patients.
Evidence: CD14+ macrophages/monocytes were particularly high in adipose tissue and around necrotic areas
vast amounts of sCD14 shed from those cells were revealed
S100+ dDC were distributed throughout diffuse mixed infiltrates with increasing density towards the margins of necrotic areas
dendritic cell appendices reached into the damaged tissue
In contrast to granulomas, regions of diffuse cellular infiltration contained sparsely distributed elastase+ PMNL
278.Rationale: The passage describes CD14+ macrophages and dendritic cells (S100+ dDC) present in diffuse infiltrates within necrotic areas, separate from granulomas. In immunology, these cells are involved in antigen presentation. Their presence in areas without granuloma formation suggests that antigen presentation can occur independently of organized granulomas. This is supported by the histological findings in antibiotic-treated patients with effective immune responses, indicating a role for antigen presentation in diverse tissue contexts.

278.Option: The development of highly organized structures, such as granulomas, is not required for the resorption of destroyed tissue, which is consistent with the observed lack of major inflammatory symptoms in the enrolled patients.
Evidence: highly organized epithelioid granulomas of different size and state of differentiation, primarily located in deeper dermal tissue
In the other four patients massive cellular infiltration was observed
Histopathological characteristics of BU were still found in all four patients
278.Rationale: The passage describes granulomas and other organized structures in responding patients, but does not assess whether they are required for tissue resorption. Additionally, there is no description of inflammatory symptoms in the enrolled patients; symptoms are only mentioned in the background section. The inference about 'lack of major inflammatory symptoms' is not supported by evidence from the study participants. The passage focuses on histopathology, not clinical symptoms, so this conclusion cannot be derived.

279.Option: The rising number of leishmaniasis cases in Austria is primarily driven by increased international travel to endemic regions, particularly among younger populations who are more vulnerable to infection.
Evidence: In 83 (56.8%) of the 146 CL cases, the travel history was available and 48 countries or regions were visited prior to development of disease.
Primarily children, adolescents and adults up to the age of 30 were affected.
279.Rationale: The passage shows that travel history is common in CL cases and that younger age groups are affected, but it does not establish that increased travel is the primary driver of the rising case numbers. Other factors such as asylum applications, climate changes, and post-pandemic travel resumption are also mentioned. Additionally, while younger populations are more affected, the passage does not attribute this to greater vulnerability to infection; it may be due to exposure patterns rather than inherent susceptibility.

279.Option: The emergence of congenital leishmaniasis in Austria indicates that local sandfly vectors are now capable of transmitting the L. donovani/infantum complex, leading to autochthonous transmission.
Evidence: Based on the patient´s history and findings, a diagnosis of vertically transmitted concurrent VL and diffuse CL was made.
Austria, which is considered non-endemic for leishmaniasis.
279.Rationale: The congenital leishmaniasis case was caused by vertical transmission from a mother who was seropositive after traveling to an endemic region, not by local sandfly vectors. The passage does not provide evidence of autochthonous transmission via sandflies in Austria; instead, it emphasizes that Austria is non-endemic and cases are largely imported or vertically transmitted.

279.Option: The high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies in Austrian military personnel indicates that leishmaniasis is now endemic in Austria due to widespread asymptomatic transmission.
Evidence: a relatively high seroprevalence of anti-Leishmania antibodies was shown in several serological surveillance studies conducted in Austrian military personnel.
Austria, which is considered non-endemic for leishmaniasis.
279.Rationale: While seroprevalence is high in military personnel, the passage explicitly states that Austria is non-endemic for leishmaniasis. High seroprevalence may indicate past exposure or asymptomatic infections, but it does not demonstrate endemicity, which requires ongoing local transmission. The passage attributes cases primarily to travel-related exposures, not widespread local transmission.

279.Option: The increasing detection of L. infantum in cutaneous leishmaniasis cases in Austria suggests that the species has developed greater virulence and transmissibility in human hosts over time.
Evidence: L. infantum was identified in 4 cases (4.0%) and two of these had occurred in 2021.

The rise could be explained by epidemiological factors like travel patterns, changes in diagnostic methods, or increased exposure, without implying evolution of the parasite.

The trend is specifically highlighted as increasing since 2016, reinforcing the evolving nature of the disease in this region.

281.Option: The lack of association between malaria infection and low birth weight in this study suggests that placental histopathological changes are not clinically relevant in this setting.

However, when the women who experienced more than one infection during their pregnancy were compared to uninfected women, there was a small but significant increase in both the thickness of the placental barrier (median [IQR]) 4.52 [4.25, 5.12] vs 4.17 [3.56, 6.61], p = 0.023 and the percentage of intervillous monocytes 3.13 [2.19, 4.22] vs 2.02 [1.20, 3.12], p = 0.025 (Table 2).
281.Rationale: The passage shows no significant difference in birth weight between infected and uninfected women, and no difference in placental histopathology for all infected women versus uninfected. However, for women with multiple infections, there were significant increases in placental barrier thickness and monocytes. The absence of a birth weight difference does not allow the inference that histopathological changes are clinically irrelevant, as clinical relevance could involve other outcomes (e.g., maternal anemia, long-term child health) not assessed here. The passage does not evaluate clinical relevance of the histopathological changes, so this inference cannot be derived.

281.Option: The increase in placental barrier thickness observed in women with multiple malaria infections may be primarily driven by P. vivax due to its higher prevalence in the region, despite the lack of parasite sequestration in the placenta.

We are beginning to understand some of the basic pathological processes associated with P. vivax during pregnancy and have recently shown that the placentas of women exposed to P. vivax during pregnancy have increased barrier thickness, syncytial knots and monocyte numbers compared to uninfected placentas, even in the absence of evidence for parasite sequestration (a hallmark for P. falciparum-associated pathology) [25].
281.Rationale: The passage directly states that P. vivax is more prevalent in the region and that the increase in placental barrier thickness in women with multiple infections is at least partially driven by P. vivax. There is no evidence that the ratio counteracts barrier thickening; indeed, the observed associations suggest the opposite. Thus, the inference is not supported by the passage.

282.Option: The high cure rates observed in the complete case analysis, particularly for the Milt+PM regimen, suggest that the treatment regimens are more effective in patients with less severe baseline clinical characteristics.

Although not significantly consistent, patients treated with SDA and Milt+PM (a majority of whom were treated at district hospitals) were younger, more likely to be female, and to present with severe wasting than those treated with AmB+Milt.

In fact, the passage indicates that patients with more severe conditions, such as severe wasting, were more common in the SDA and Milt+PM arms, yet these regimens still achieved high cure rates. The active tracing efforts may have contributed to compliance, but there is no evidence to infer that these regimens are 'more feasible' in primary health care settings based on compliance data alone.

animals were either found dead (wtLUJV N=1; recLUJV N=2) or euthanized when moribund (wtLUJV N=9; recLUJV N=8) 11–16 days PI.
No sham-infected control animals (N=6) developed any signs of clinical illness during the duration of the experiment.

Within 48 h PI, a dramatic rise in viral load, measured by qRT-PCR and reported as tissue culture infective dose 50 (TCID50) equivalents (eq), was detected in all tissues and fluids analyzed (whole blood: 4.64×102 eq; liver: 1.83×105 eq; spleen: 8.13×104 eq; lung: 6.22×103 eq; kidney: 5.24×103 eq; urine: 2.20×103 eq)
with high viral loads detected throughout the experiment.
liver tissues became progressively more pale and friable, and demonstrated enhanced reticular patterns of reddening suggestive of locally extensive to diffuse hepatic congestion

The most consistently observed lesions among LUJV-infected animals were hepatocyte and myocardial necrosis.
moderate to marked thrombocytopenia was observed (Fig. 3E; ranging 99–163×106 uL−1).
frank external genitourinary hemorrhage (in 1 animal)

signs of gross anatomical signs of profound coagulopathy suggestive of HF and bleeding diatheses.
Broadly, we observed no change or decrease (2 to 5-fold) in several pro-inflammatory mRNA levels (IL-1b, RANTES), and modest up-regulation of TNFa (2 to 7-fold), primarily in kidneys.
Specific gene mRNA signatures for IL-2 and iNOS were not detected in any tissue at any time.
283.Rationale: The passage provides extensive evidence that LUJV infection in strain 13/N guinea pigs causes severe, rapidly progressive disease with 100% lethality, high viral loads in multiple organs, disseminated organ damage including hepatic and myocardial necrosis, and clinical signs consistent with coagulopathy such as thrombocytopenia and hemorrhage. with high viral loads detected throughout the experiment.

The absence of specific cytokine increases does not preclude dysregulation in other aspects, and the comparison to other viruses requires external knowledge not present in the passage.

284.Option: The reduced parasite burden in ALD1 KO-infected hosts may be due to a failure in stomodeal valve colonization in sand flies, which is critical for transmission to mammalian hosts.

the parasite load was slightly but significantly (F(1; 14) = 5.28; p = 0.037) higher in the WT control- compared to the ALD1 KO-infected mice.
WT and ALD1 KO L. mexicana differ in their ability to survive in primary murine macrophages in vitro, and that this difference lies in amastigotes‘ ability to replicate intracellularly.

285.Option: The discrepancy between viral RNA detection in excreta and absence in mosquitoes indicates that the MX method is prone to false positives due to cross-reactivity with other arboviruses.
Evidence: Because this Duo assay cross-react with USUV, a second screening was performed with a WNV specific but less sensitive single target assay in second intention to discriminate a single WNV infection from a co-infection with both viruses when needed.
285.Rationale: The passage addresses cross-reactivity by using specific assays to discriminate between viruses, but it does not attribute the discrepancy between excreta and mosquito testing to false positives from cross-reactivity. The measures described are to ensure accuracy, not evidence that the MX method is prone to false positives due to cross-reactivity. The discrepancy is likely due to other factors such as sensitivity differences or timing of infection.

285.Option: The detection of WNV and USUV in mosquito excreta despite the absence of viral RNA in the trapped mosquitoes suggests that the viruses are being shed into the environment through non-vector mechanisms, such as environmental contamination.
Evidence: Once infected by a virus, a single mosquito can shed between 3 to 5 log10 of viral RNA per day [27].

In MX, trapped mosquitoes, here used as environmental samplers, are kept alive in the field over several days, which (i) allows the time between trap collections to be extended and (ii) increases the likelihood of virus shedding by trapped infected mosquitoes.
285.Rationale: The passage explains that viral RNA in excreta comes from infected mosquitoes shedding the virus, not from non-vector mechanisms like environmental contamination. There is no evidence in the passage supporting environmental contamination as a source for the viral RNA in excreta. The MX method is designed to capture shedding from mosquitoes, and the discrepancy may arise from factors like mosquito infection status or detection sensitivity.

285.Option: The detection of WNV and USUV in mosquito excreta, even when no viral RNA was found in the mosquitoes themselves, suggests that predatory arthropods such as ants may have contaminated the excreta, highlighting a potential limitation of the MX method.
285.Rationale: The passage does not mention predatory arthropods, ants, or contamination from external sources as a limitation of the MX method. The method involves collecting excreta from trapped mosquitoes in a controlled setup, and no such contamination is discussed. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

285.Option: The high rate of viral RNA detection in mosquito excreta (75%) indicates that the MX method is more sensitive than traditional entomological surveillance for detecting cryptic enzootic arbovirus circulation.
Evidence: WNV and USUV were detected in trapped mosquito excreta at high rate. A total of 52 excreta samples, obtained from 13 different sites in the region of Nouvelle-Aquitaine between the end of July and August 2023, were collected and processed (Fig 1). WNV or USUV RNA was detected in 39 (75%) excreta, alone or in combination.

Both the high proportion of asymptomatic infections caused by WNV and USUV and their cryptic enzootic circulation make their detection in the environment challenging. There is currently no cost-effective and easy-to-use method for the early detection of the circulation of these viruses, which is essential for triggering the systematic viral RNA screening of blood and organ donors for these viruses.

Detecting arboviruses in field-collected mosquitoes is primarily used worldwide as an early warning system for viral circulation and to assess the risk of human transmission [20]. In most cases, arbovirus surveillance involves pooling mosquitoes by species, date, and location before extracting and detecting viral genomic RNA. For WNV surveillance, thousands to hundreds of thousands of mosquitoes are typically collected over a season, leading to pools of 50 to 200 mosquitoes per sample to minimize costs, with reported minimum infection rates ranging from less than 2% [21–24] to 10% [24,25] across studies. Here, at the interface between entomological and environmental surveillance, we have implemented a non-invasive molecular xenomonitoring (MX) approach that uses trapped mosquito excreta to monitor the emergence and circulation of WNV and USUV in real time.
However, no USUV RNA was detected in mosquitoes from collections C3, C4 and C5, although viral RNA from both viruses had previously been detected in excreta.

The overall positivity rate by PCR was 5% and 3% for WNV and USUV, respectively. These results may overestimate positivity rates, as we only tested mosquitoes from traps for which a PCR-positive result was obtained for WNV or USUV in the corresponding excreta.
285.Rationale: The passage directly states that MX detected viral RNA in 75% of excreta samples, highlighting its high detection rate. It also emphasizes the challenge of detecting cryptic enzootic circulation and positions MX as a novel, real-time surveillance method. The discrepancy where excreta is positive but mosquitoes are negative (e.g., in collections C3, C4, C5) suggests that excreta testing can indicate viral presence even when individual mosquito testing does not, implying higher sensitivity. Compared to traditional entomological surveillance with lower infection rates and pooling methods, MX appears more effective for early detection, as supported by the evidence.

285.Option: The presence of viral RNA in mosquito excreta without detection in the mosquitoes themselves suggests that the viruses are capable of replicating in the digestive tract of mosquitoes, indicating a new mode of transmission.
Evidence: Once infected by a virus, a single mosquito can shed between 3 to 5 log10 of viral RNA per day [27].
285.Rationale: The passage explains that infected mosquitoes shed viral RNA in excreta as part of known viral replication and shedding processes. The absence of viral RNA in mosquitoes when excreta is positive does not imply a new replication site or transmission mode; it could be due to factors like clearance of infection or detection limits. The passage does not suggest any novel replication mechanisms or transmission modes based on this discrepancy.
